decisionGuides.question: Choose one value from a known set

when: The number of options is small and should stay visible.
Fast switching matters more than compactness.
The choices can stay in a dropdown.
Search is not required.
The user needs filtering or type-to-search.
The option list can become long.
All options should be visible with classic form semantics.
You need explicit radio semantics in a form.
The choice should be presented as richer rows with description or metadata.
The options are hierarchical.
The value is the application language or locale.
Each language should show a country flag beside its name.
Selected values should collapse into tags.
The user needs filtering and a compact control.
The option set is small and visible.
A segmented-control style is appropriate.
notes: Set `selectionMode="multiple"`.

when: The options can stay visible as a checklist.
Multi-selection must work across a hierarchy.
Set `selectionMode="multiple"`.

decisionGuides.question: Show contextual or blocking UI above the page

when: The content is a short label or hint.
The panel should not be interactive.
The content is anchored, custom, and non-blocking.
The panel can contain controls.
You need a lightweight action menu or menu-like list.
The menu is triggered by right click or context action.
The task needs a larger side panel but should keep page context visible.
The task is blocking and requires focused attention.
The user is confirming or canceling a destructive or high-risk action.

decisionGuides.question: Navigate somewhere vs. perform an action

when: Activating it changes the URL — an external link, a route, or an in-page anchor.
It should be openable in a new tab, copyable, and crawlable.
Activating it runs behavior on the current page — submit, open an overlay, delete, toggle.
It needs sizes, action variants, or a loading state.
notes: Use `variant="ghost"` when an action needs to look understated like a link.

when: The links are app-section navigation inside a shell or sidebar.
The links express where the current page sits in a hierarchy.
The message should stay visible in the page layout.
The message is ephemeral and should not shift layout.
A root `TToastProvider` is already mounted.
The user needs a purposeful zero-state, not only a status message.
You need a determinate progress indicator.
You need indeterminate loading feedback.
You want loading placeholders that preserve layout.
The story is a single headline number, optionally with a trend and delta.
It is not a plot — prefer the number over a chart when one value says it.
You want an inline micro-trend next to a value, in a table cell, or in text.
No axes, legend, or tooltip are needed.
You need line, area, or bar plots with axes, gridlines, legend, and hover tooltip.
There are one or more series over shared categories.
The data is a part-of-whole breakdown (donut or pie).
You are showing completion toward a single target, not a distribution or trend.

recipes.name: Field with text input
useWhen: A form control needs label, hint, and validation messaging.
notes: Match `htmlFor` on `TFormField` with the child `id`.
Mirror invalid state onto `TInput` when error text is shown.

recipes.name: Searchable multi-choice field
useWhen: A form needs compact multi-select with filtering.
notes: Pass option keywords when search should match hidden synonyms.
Use `maxVisibleTags` to avoid oversized triggers.

recipes.name: Scheduling pair
useWhen: A form needs start and end scheduling controls.
notes: Keep both values in `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm` format.
Set end `min` from the selected start time when appropriate.

recipes.name: Full-viewport application shell
useWhen: The app needs a header plus side navigation that fills the whole screen and adapts to mobile.
The layout must use the full page width on large monitors, not a centered column.
notes: `TAppShell` orchestrates the header, sidebar, and fluid content in one component; put a `TNavMenu` in its `sidebar` slot and it inherits collapsed state automatically.
Leave `mobile` unset to auto-switch at `breakpoint` (default 768px), or bind it to force mobile; the sidebar becomes an off-canvas drawer with a header menu button.
Mount it at the app root. Wrap only inner regions in `TContainer` if a specific area needs a constrained reading width.

recipes.name: Sidebar application shell
useWhen: The screen needs persistent section navigation and you are composing the shell by hand.
notes: Let `TNavMenu` inherit collapsed state from `TSidebar` when possible.
Prefer `TAppShell` (recipe `app-shell`) when you also need full-viewport sizing and a responsive mobile drawer.

recipes.name: Product brand lockup in a shell header
useWhen: A shell header needs a logo plus a product name (and optional subtitle) that stays aligned as the sidebar collapses.
notes: Put the lockup in the `header-start` slot, not `header`. Filling `header-start` switches the header to the shell's own grid, so the mark tracks `--tree-app-shell-sidebar-width` and animates with the collapse — no local column math.
The slot is scoped: use its `collapsed` binding to drop the wordmark and keep only the mark (`v-if="!collapsed"`), rather than hiding text with CSS.

Logo is `TAvatar` (`size`, `alt`, `initials` fallback) or a plain `` sized with `--tree-size-icon-*`; name and subtitle are `TText` (`weight`, `tone="muted"`, `truncate`) — never raw ``/`` with a local class.

TreeUI ships no `TBrand` component on purpose: the logo asset, the product name, and what survives collapse are product identity, which must stay in the consumer. See DECISIONS.md → Portability Boundary.
Company and service logos (AWS, LocalStack, social networks) are brand assets and are not TreeUI icons — source them from the app's brand policy, not the icon registry.

recipes.name: Icons on navigation items
useWhen: Navigation items each carry an icon.
The navigation collapses to an icon-only rail, so the icon is the only thing left identifying a section.
Some of the icons the app needs are not TreeUI built-ins.
notes: Set `icon` on the item, as a registered NAME: `{ label: 'Dashboard', value: 'dashboard', icon: 'layout-dashboard' }`. Do not import an icon component per item, and do not put a `TIcon` in the `#item` slot just to get an icon — the item renders it for you.

`TNavMenuItem.icon` is `TIconInput`, so a component is accepted too. Prefer the name: `items` normally lives in a `ref`, a `computed`, or a route config, and a string is never made reactive — a component object there needs `markRaw` or Vue warns.

An item with no icon (or an unregistered name) falls back to a letter marker, and a `console.warn` lists the valid names — once per name, in production builds too. Give every item an icon before shipping a collapsible rail, or the collapsed state is a column of letters.

Icons the app needs but TreeUI does not ship are REGISTERED, not worked around: one `registerTreeIcons` call in `main.ts` and they are usable by name everywhere. Never hand-roll an `<svg>` — without the `absoluteStrokeWidth` correction its stroke scales with the icon (1.67px at size 20, 4px at 48), so it looks bolder than its neighbours at every size above 24.

Check the name is not already taken before registering it — `hasTreeIcon(name)` answers that. Registering one of the 364 built-in names replaces that built-in everywhere, silently, which is a supported swap but never what you want when the goal was adding an icon.
Keep the icon vocabulary consistent: built-ins use TreeUI's descriptive kebab-case names, so register custom ones with the same convention.
Icon registration is process-wide state. In component tests that register icons, call `resetTreeIcons()` in an `afterEach` so one case's icons do not leak into the next.
Set `to` for the route target on each item — `TNavMenuItem` has no `href` and no `id`; identity is `value`.
This recipe covers the icons only; the surrounding layout is its own recipe (`app-shell` or `sidebar-shell`).

recipes.name: Dashboard metric grid
useWhen: A dashboard needs KPI cards.
notes: Use `TCard` only when the stat needs additional arbitrary content.

recipes.name: Color field
useWhen: A form needs a color selection control without adding a dedicated TreeUI component.
notes: Wrap the native color input with `TFormField` for label, hint, and error handling.

recipes.name: Tag input field
useWhen: A form needs freeform tag entry such as domains, labels, or emails.
notes: Keep the draft value in app state and add a tag on Enter or separator keys.
Use removable `TTag` items below or inside the field wrapper.

recipes.name: Data table with paging
useWhen: A structured list needs external paging controls.
notes: `TTable` stays focused on structured listing; filters and toolbars compose around it.
Use `#cell-{key}` slots when a column renders derived or rich content.

recipes.name: Router-backed tabs
useWhen: Tabs should deep-link to app state without turning into primary route navigation.
notes: Bind `v-model` to a route param, query param, or derived route name in the app layer.
Prefer `TNavMenu` when the selection becomes app-section navigation instead of in-place panel switching.

recipes.name: Destructive confirmation flow
useWhen: The user is confirming a high-risk action.
notes: Use `TToast` only for the post-confirm success feedback.

recipes.name: Chart surface
useWhen: A screen needs data visualization inside a framed, titled surface.
notes: Prefer the native TChart / TDonutChart / TSparkline — no external charting library is required.
Use TreeUI for the surrounding surface, headings, badges, range filters, and empty/loading states.
TChart has its own loading and empty slots; you can still wrap it in TCard for the frame.
Only reach for an external chart engine for chart types TreeUI does not cover yet (e.g. scatter, heatmap).

recipes.name: Metric dashboard
useWhen: A dashboard shows KPIs alongside trends and breakdowns.
notes: Lead with TStat tiles; embed a TSparkline in a stat's meta slot for the inline trend.
Use one TChart for the main time series and a TDonutChart for a part-of-whole split.
Keep series colors stable across the dashboard — rely on the --tree-color-chart-* order, do not remap by rank.

recipes.name: Anchored help affordance
useWhen: A trigger needs short or medium contextual help.
notes: Use `TTooltip` for short, non-interactive help.
Escalate to `TPopover` when the content becomes interactive.

recipes.name: Pricing comparison section
useWhen: Plans should be compared side by side.
notes: Use `highlighted` and `badge` on the recommended plan.

recipes.name: Hierarchical selection
useWhen: Selection works across nested groups or folders.
notes: Use `selectionMode="multiple"` when permissions or tag assignment spans nested nodes.

recipes.name: Application language switcher
useWhen: The app lets the user pick a language or locale, with an optional flag per option.
notes: The app owns the language list: TreeUI ships no locale dataset, so pass `options` with your own `label`, `value`, and optional `code` and `description`.

`TLanguageSelect` only emits the chosen `value` through `update:modelValue`; the app performs every locale side effect (i18n locale, persistence, `document.documentElement.lang`, route or API updates).
Set `code` only where a flag is meaningful — a language is not a country — and omit it to render that option without a flag.
Set `flagBaseUrl` once, at the switcher, when flag assets are self-hosted; it is forwarded to every nested `TFlag`.
Wrap with `TFormField` when the switcher sits in a form and needs a label, hint, or error text; drop the wrapper for a bare toolbar or header control.

practices.title: Visual interaction feedback
practices.summary: Every interaction answers back. Hover, focus, active, loading, and disabled are designed states — never afterthoughts left to the consumer.
rules: Interactive surfaces ship hover, active, and focus-visible treatments out of the box.
Loading states announce themselves (aria-busy plus a labelled, localizable spinner), replace the resting glyph instead of stacking beside it, and block duplicate activation.
Disabled and loading elements never emit activation events.
Focus indicators stay visible in both light and dark themes.

practices.title: Accessible by default
practices.summary: Native semantics first, ARIA where the pattern requires it, full keyboard support, and focus management the consumer never has to write.
rules: Native elements before roles; role="dialog", aria-modal, aria-labelledby only where semantics require them, and no composite-widget role (grid, tree) is claimed without its full keyboard model.
Tab/Shift+Tab order, Escape to close, Enter/Space to activate, arrow keys where the pattern expects them.
Overlays trap focus while open and restore it on close.
Clickable targets stay at or above 44×44px.

practices.title: One predictable API
practices.summary: The same contracts everywhere: one size scale, one action-variant scale, v-model for form controls, open/close for overlays. Learn one component and you know them all.
rules: Sizes are sm | md | lg across the library.
Action variants come from one scale: solid, outline, ghost, soft, danger.
Form controls speak modelValue + update:modelValue (v-model).
Overlays speak open/defaultOpen + update:open/open-change.

practices.title: Empty, loading, and error are designed states
practices.summary: Screens communicate status honestly: skeletons while loading, purposeful empty states, and status surfaces on one semantic tone scale.
rules: Default, hover, active, focus, disabled, loading, and error/empty states are designed and documented — not just the happy path.
Status tones map to semantic tokens (success, warning, error, info) and pass both themes.
Async feedback is immediate: skeleton or spinner first, toast or alert on completion.

practices.title: Alignment without custom CSS
practices.summary: Layout primitives absorb spacing, alignment, and rhythm so product code never hand-rolls the same flexbox again.
rules: Spacing comes from the --tree-space-* scale, applied by layout components.
Compose TPage, TStack, TGrid, and TSplit before writing a custom layout div.
Forms align through TFormField + TStack + TGrid — labels, hints, and errors line up by contract.
Reading widths and page gutters come from TContainer/TPage, not local max-widths.

practices.title: Token-driven clarity
practices.summary: No raw hex, px, or font values anywhere. Every visual decision references a --tree-* token, so light/dark and brand theming stay coherent by construction.
rules: Zero hardcoded colors, spacing, or typography in component code.
Light and dark are first-class themes; every state passes both.
Themes switch by attribute (data-tree-theme) with no component changes.

guidance: Use the plugin when global registration is preferred.
Use named imports when tree-shaken local imports fit the consumer app better.
Prefer `T<Name>` imports in all new code.

globalRules: Always import `@treeui/vue/style.css` once in the consuming application.
All components use the `T<Name>` export prefix; there are no `Tree<Name>` aliases.
Overlay-style components generally support controlled and uncontrolled open state.

required: Mount one `TToastProvider` near the application root before calling `useToast()` from feature components.
notes: Use `useToast()` for programmatic notifications.
Choose provider `position` once per app shell unless a feature has a strong reason to differ.

required: Wrap a control such as `TInput`, `TSelect`, `TCombobox`, `TCheckbox`, or `TDatePicker`.
Pass `htmlFor` matching the child control `id` when the child exposes one.
notes: Set the child control invalid state separately when `error` is present.
Pass `options` as `{ label, value, disabled? }[]` where `value` can be `string` or `number`.
Use `placeholder` when no selection has been made.
Pass `options` as labeled items and keep labels human-readable because search matches visible fields.
Pass `modelValue` as `string[]`.
Option search checks `label`, `description`, and `keywords`.
Use `maxVisibleTags` to control trigger density in compact layouts.

required: Pass `code` as an ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code. Matching is case-insensitive — the value is trimmed and lowercased internally.
Allow `https://flagcdn.com` in the app `Content-Security-Policy` `img-src` directive, or point `baseUrl` at a self-hosted mirror — flag images are fetched from a third-party CDN at runtime.
notes: A mirror must serve `{baseUrl}/{segment}/{code}.png` for both steps of every size: 1x `h20` (sm), `h20` (md), `h24` (lg); 2x `h40` (sm), `h40` (md), `h60` (lg). The 2x asset is emitted through `srcset`.
Those fixed-height segments are part of the contract, and they are the CDN's flat artwork — a mirror must serve flat flags, not the waving ones.
Do not substitute the CDN's ratio-normalised width-by-height endpoints. They equalise aspect ratio only by switching to the waving artwork, which reads as decoration next to flat UI icons.
The fixed-height assets keep each flag's true proportions — Nepal is a pennant, Switzerland is square, Qatar is elongated. A shared height, not a shared ratio, is what aligns a column of flags.
CSS puts the image in a fixed 3:2 box (3:2 being the most common national flag ratio) with `object-fit: contain`, so outliers centre inside the box instead of being cropped or distorted.
Each retina segment is at least twice its box height — boxes are `--tree-size-icon-sm/md/lg` = 16px/20px/24px — so 2x screens never upscale.
`baseUrl` may be written with or without a trailing slash; trailing slashes are stripped before the path is built. An unknown `size` falls back to the `md` assets.
When the image cannot load — offline, blocked by CSP, unknown code — the component renders the uppercased `code` as text. Override that text with `fallbackText`.
The flag is `aria-hidden` by default. Pass `label` only when the flag carries meaning on its own; beside a visible country or language name it should stay decorative.

required: Pass `options` as `{ label, value, code?, description?, disabled? }[]`. TreeUI ships no language, locale, or country dataset — the app owns its language list.
Apply locale side effects (i18n locale, `lang` attribute, persistence) in the app layer; the component only reports the selected `value`.
Pass an `aria-label` whenever `iconOnly` is set — the visible language name is gone, so it is the control's only accessible name. It also labels the listbox.
notes: `variant` is `field` (default) or `switcher`, typed as `TLanguageSelectVariant` and exported from `@treeui/vue`.

Use `variant="field"` inside a form, next to other inputs, or wrapped in `TFormField`: the flag leads the trigger, the trigger fills its container, and `width` applies. A visible field label already says what the control changes.

Use `variant="switcher"` for a page-level control in a navbar or app bar, where nothing nearby labels it. A translate icon (`TIcon name="languages"`) leads and the current flag trails before the chevron, so the control reads as "page language, now set to this" without an external label.
A switcher sizes to its content, so `width` does not apply, and its listbox aligns to the trigger's right edge.
`iconOnly` is switcher-only and drops the visible language name, leaving the translate icon and the current flag.
`value` is an opaque application key, typically a BCP-47 tag such as `pt-BR`. TreeUI never parses or validates it.
`code` is the ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code used for the option flag. Omit it to render the option without a flag — a language is not a country, so many entries have no honest flag.
Use `description` for a secondary line such as the endonym.
Set `flagBaseUrl` once on the select rather than per option; it is forwarded to every nested `TFlag`.
Bind `modelValue` to a `string`, and use `v-model:open` when the app controls open state.
The CDN and Content-Security-Policy requirements under `TFlag` apply whenever any option carries a `code`.
Use `selectionMode="single"` for segmented single choice.
Use `selectionMode="multiple"` for small visible multi-choice sets.
Bind `modelValue` to a `YYYY-MM-DD` string.
Use `min` and `max` with the same format.
Bind `modelValue` to a `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm` string.
Use `step` in seconds. Values divisible by 60 produce minute increments.
Use `min` and `max` in the same date-time format.
Bind `modelValue` to `File[]`.
Use `accept`, `multiple`, and size limits in the app layer to constrain uploads.
Mount at the app root; it fills the viewport (100dvh, full width) and scrolls its content region independently.
Put navigation in the `sidebar` slot (e.g. `TNavMenu`) — it inherits collapsed state from the shell provider like `TSidebar`.
Leave `mobile` unset to auto-switch to an off-canvas drawer at `breakpoint`, or bind `mobile` to control it explicitly.
Use `v-model:collapsed` for the desktop rail and `v-model:sidebarOpen` for the mobile drawer when the app persists layout state.
Pair with `TNavMenu` for app-section navigation.
Use controlled `collapsed` state when the app shell persists layout preferences.
Prefer `TAppShell` when you also want full-viewport sizing, a top header, and a responsive mobile drawer.
When nested inside `TSidebar` or `TAppShell`, `collapsed` can be inherited from the sidebar provider.
Pass `items` as `{ label, value, icon?, to?, badge?, description?, shortLabel?, disabled?, exact? }[]`.
`icon` is `TIconInput` — a registered icon name (`icon: 'layout-dashboard'`) or a component. Use the name: it needs no import, and no `markRaw` when the item list is reactive.
An item with no icon, or with a name that is not registered, shows a letter marker instead. That marker is also all a collapsed rail can show, so give every item an icon when the sidebar collapses.
Register any icon the app needs beyond the built-ins first — see `icons` above.

required: Use a registered name: one of the built-ins, or one the app added with `registerTreeIcons`. See `icons` above.
notes: `size` is a pixel count, not `sm | md | lg`. Passing `size="md"` renders an unsized svg.
The icon is `aria-hidden` by default. Pass `label` only when the icon carries meaning on its own; beside visible text it should stay decorative.
Use `nodes` shaped as `{ id, label, description?, meta?, disabled?, children? }[]`.
Control `expanded` separately when the parent app needs to persist open branches.
Pass explicit `status` per item only when the app should override the computed complete/current/upcoming logic.
Pass `plans` matching the same core fields used by `TPricingCard`.
Use `v-model:open` for the idiomatic Vue controlled pattern.
Use `href` for native links.
Use `to` when the app has vue-router and breadcrumb items should render RouterLink.
Pass `series` as `{ name, data, color? }[]` and `labels` as a parallel `string[]` (one label per data index).
Set `type` to `line`, `area`, or `bar`; use `stacked` only with `bar`.
Series colors come from `--tree-color-chart-1..8` in order; pass `color` per series to override — do not remap colors when the series set changes.
Pass `valueFormat: (n) => string` to format axis and tooltip values (currency, compact, etc.).
No charting library or provider is required — the SVG and geometry are built in.
Pass `data` as `number[]`; there are no axes, legend, or tooltip by design.
Size it with `width`/`height`; use it inside `TStat`, table cells, or text.
Always keep a visible number nearby — a sparkline supplements a value, it does not replace it.
Pass `segments` as `{ label, value, color? }[]`; non-positive values are dropped.
Set `thickness: 0` for a full pie; keep the default for a donut with a center readout.
Use the `center` slot (donut only) for a custom center; otherwise it shows the total or the hovered slice.

components.name: TAccordion
components.purpose: Organized content sections with expandable/collapsible behavior. Supports single-open and multi-open modes.

props.name: type
props.description: single – at most one item open at a time (opening one closes the rest). multiple – any number of items may be open simultaneously.

props.name: modelValue
props.description: Controlled open state. For type="single" pass a string (the item value). For type="multiple" pass an array of strings. The accordion treats itself as controlled only while modelValue !== undefined; passing undefined leaves it uncontrolled and it falls back to its internal state.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: Initial open state for uncontrolled usage. Read once when the accordion is created — changing it after mount has no effect. Ignored while modelValue is set. Do not combine it with modelValue: see notes.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disables all items. OR-ed with each item's own disabled prop, so an item is disabled when either is true. Provided to items as a computed ref, so flipping it after mount updates every item.

props.name: collapsible
props.description: Only applies when type="single". When true, the open item can be collapsed by clicking its trigger again, which emits undefined. When false, clicking the already-open trigger is a no-op and emits nothing. In type="multiple" any item can always be collapsed and this prop is ignored.

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted when the open state changes (v-model support). type="single" emits the open item value as a string, or undefined when it is collapsed. type="multiple" always emits a string[] of the open values.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Accepts TAccordionItem children.

useWhen: Sections expand and collapse inside the page flow.
Multiple disclosure items share one layout.
avoidWhen: You need tabbed navigation or route-level navigation.

alternatives.when: Only one panel should be active and the tab labels must stay visible.
The content is hierarchical rather than just collapsible.

components.name: TAlert
components.purpose: Inline status banner for info, success, warning, and danger messages.

props.name: variant
props.description: Semantic tone. Sets the --tree-alert-* custom properties that colour the surface, border, icon, and dismiss button.

props.name: size
props.description: Adjusts padding and font size only. md is the baseline defined on .t-alert itself.

props.name: dismissible
props.description: Renders the trailing dismiss button. The button is the only thing gated by this prop; the alert never hides itself.

events.name: dismiss
events.description: Emitted when the dismiss button is clicked. TAlert is stateless — it does not hide, unmount, or animate itself out. The consumer owns removal (v-if, a list splice, or a store flag); without a handler the button is inert.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Alert message content, rendered inside .t-alert__content.

slots.name: icon
slots.description: Optional leading icon. The .t-alert__icon wrapper span is rendered only when the slot is provided (v-if="$slots.icon") and is marked aria-hidden, so the icon is decorative — never put meaning there that is not also in the message text. The wrapper inherits the variant colour via --tree-alert-accent.

slots.name: dismiss-icon
slots.description: Replaces the default 16x16 × SVG inside the dismiss button. Rendered only when dismissible is true, since the slot lives inside the button. Has a working default, so it is optional. The button keeps its aria-label="Dismiss" regardless of content.

a11y: dismiss_button_hit_area: 44x44 via a .t-alert__dismiss::after pseudo-element; the visible button is 1.5rem.
keyboard: Tab to dismiss button, Enter/Space to activate

live_region_caveat: role="alert" is an assertive live region. An alert that is already in the DOM on first paint is generally not announced — mount it dynamically (v-if / list insert) when you need the message read out.
behavior: size_md_emits_class_without_css_rule: t-alert--md is always emitted but carries no CSS rule of its own; .t-alert is the md baseline and only --sm and --lg override padding and font size.

useWhen: Feedback should stay visible in-flow.
The message may contain actions or supporting content.
avoidWhen: The feedback should disappear automatically.

alternatives.when: The message is ephemeral and should not affect layout.
The screen has no data and needs stronger guidance.

components.name: TAppShell
components.purpose: Full-viewport application shell with a top header, a persistent side navigation, and a fluid content area that collapses the sidebar into an off-canvas drawer on small screens.

props.name: as
props.note: Tag or component rendered as the shell root. The root is a plain `div` by default and is therefore not a landmark — the header/sidebar/main regions carry the semantics.

props.name: sidebarWidth
props.note: Any CSS length. Sets `--tree-app-shell-sidebar-width` on the root, which drives the desktop rail track and also caps the mobile drawer at `min(100%, sidebarWidth)`.

props.name: collapsedWidth
props.note: Any CSS length. Sets `--tree-app-shell-collapsed-width` on the root, but it only takes effect while the root carries `is-collapsed` (desktop + `collapsible` + collapsed).

props.name: collapsible
props.note: Master gate for the desktop rail. While false the root never gets `is-collapsed`, the built-in collapse toggle is not rendered, and `toggleCollapsed` is a no-op.

props.name: collapsed
props.note: Controlled desktop rail state (v-model:collapsed). Leave undefined for uncontrolled. When set, the component only emits — the parent must apply the new value.

props.name: defaultCollapsed
props.note: Initial value for uncontrolled use. Read once during setup; later changes to the prop are ignored.

props.name: mobile
props.note: Forces mobile mode when explicitly true or false. Leave undefined to auto-detect from `breakpoint`.

props.name: breakpoint
props.note: Any CSS length (px/rem/em). The drawer boundary: below this width the sidebar becomes the off-canvas drawer. Measured on the shell root via ResizeObserver (not the viewport, not the content panel — measuring the root avoids the feedback loop the panel would cause as the rail collapses). Only consulted while `mobile` is undefined.

props.name: railBreakpoint
props.note: Any CSS length, greater than `breakpoint`. Enables the three-state responsive policy: below `breakpoint` → drawer; between `breakpoint` and `railBreakpoint` → the sidebar is auto-forced to the collapsed rail; at/above `railBreakpoint` → the user's manual collapse preference applies. Omit for the binary drawer↔expanded behavior. Auto-forcing the rail does NOT mutate the manual preference and emits no update:collapsed/collapse-change — the preference returns when the width grows back past railBreakpoint.

props.name: sidebarOpen
props.note: Controlled off-canvas drawer state (v-model:sidebarOpen). Mobile-only — the flag is tracked in desktop mode but nothing renders from it.

props.name: defaultSidebarOpen
props.note: Initial value for uncontrolled use. Read once during setup.

props.name: immersive
props.note: Controlled immersive state (v-model:immersive). Hides the header and sidebar so the content fills the viewport. The chrome is hidden with CSS rather than unmounted, so toggling never remounts the default slot. While immersive, the mobile drawer is not rendered.

props.name: defaultImmersive
props.note: Initial value for uncontrolled use. Read once during setup.

props.name: closeOnEscape
props.note: Drawer only. Escape is handled on the drawer surface, so it fires only while focus is inside the drawer.

props.name: closeOnOverlay
props.note: Drawer only. Set false to keep the drawer open on backdrop clicks.

props.name: showMenuButton
props.note: Renders the built-in hamburger in the header. Mobile only. Set false to supply your own trigger and call the `toggleSidebar` slot prop from the `header` or `header-start` slot.

props.name: showCollapseButton
props.note: Renders the built-in collapse toggle in the sidebar footer. Desktop only, and only when `collapsible` is set — no need to wire your own. Never rendered in the mobile drawer.

props.name: collapseLabel
props.note: aria-label of the collapse toggle while the rail is expanded.

props.name: expandLabel
props.note: aria-label of the collapse toggle while the rail is collapsed.

props.name: menuLabel
props.note: aria-label of the built-in hamburger. Static — it does not swap to a close label while the drawer is open.

props.name: closeLabel
props.note: aria-label of the drawer's close button.

props.name: sidebarLabel
props.note: aria-label applied to both the desktop <aside> and the drawer <aside>.

events.name: update:collapsed
events.description: Desktop rail collapsed state changed. Enables v-model:collapsed.

events.name: collapse-change
events.description: Same change as update:collapsed, for non-v-model usage. Both always fire together.

events.name: update:sidebarOpen
events.description: Off-canvas drawer open state changed. Enables v-model:sidebarOpen.

events.name: sidebar-open-change
events.description: Same change as update:sidebarOpen, for non-v-model usage. Both always fire together.

events.name: update:immersive
events.description: Immersive state changed. Enables v-model:immersive.

events.name: immersive-change
events.description: Same change as update:immersive, for non-v-model usage. Both always fire together.

slots.name: header
slots.description: Main header region. Spans the full header width by default; once `header-start` is filled it is constrained to the content column so it lines up with the panel underneath. On mobile the header falls back to a single flex row either way.
slots.note: AppShellSlotProps — { mobile, collapsed, sidebarOpen, immersive, toggleSidebar, closeSidebar, toggleCollapsed, toggleImmersive }

slots.name: header-start
slots.description: Header region sitting over the sidebar rail (typically the brand mark). Filling it adds `has-start` to the header, which switches the header to the shell's own two-column grid and tracks the rail width as it collapses.

slots.name: header-end
slots.description: Trailing header region for actions/controls, pinned to the far end of the row. Filling it adds `has-end`; in the plain flex header the content region's grow already pushes it to the end, and in the `has-start` grid it becomes a content-sized third column with the trailing padding. Replaces the consumer's own `TStack justify=flex-end` wrapper inside `#header`.

slots.name: sidebar-header
slots.description: Pinned above the sidebar body, inset to line up with nav item icons. Rendered in both the desktop aside and the mobile drawer.

slots.name: sidebar
slots.description: Sidebar body — the natural home for a TNavMenu, which picks up the collapsed state through provide/inject. Rendered in both the desktop aside and the mobile drawer, and takes the vertical slack so the footer pins to the bottom.

slots.name: sidebar-footer
slots.description: Pinned to the bottom of the sidebar, inset to line up with nav item icons. In the desktop aside it shares one container with the built-in collapse toggle, which is rendered after the slot content; in the mobile drawer the container holds the slot content alone.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Main content area. Scrolls independently of the header and sidebar.
slots.note: { mobile: boolean, collapsed: boolean, immersive: boolean, toggleImmersive: () => void } — a subset of AppShellSlotProps. toggleImmersive is included because immersive hides the chrome, so the content is the only place an exit control can live.

slots.name: menu-icon
slots.description: Replaces the default hamburger glyph inside the built-in menu button. Use `sidebarOpen` to swap the icon while the drawer is open. Renders inside the button, so it must be presentational only.

slots.name: collapse-icon
slots.description: Replaces the default glyph inside the built-in collapse toggle. Renders inside the button, so it must be presentational only.

a11y: semantic_regions: <header>, <aside> and <main>; the shell root itself is a plain div and is not a landmark
role: dialog — on the mobile drawer only
focus_trap: Tab and Shift+Tab cycle inside the drawer; with no focusable descendant, focus is pinned to the drawer itself
restores_focus_on_close: only when the drawer was opened through the built-in menu button or the toggleSidebar slot prop — those capture document.activeElement first
menu_button_aria_controls_drawer: aria-controls always points at the sidebar id, and on mobile that element exists only while the drawer is open
collapse_button_aria_expanded: reflects the expanded rail, i.e. !collapsed
collapse_button_label_swaps_by_state: collapseLabel while expanded, expandLabel while collapsed
behavior: resets_body_margin_while_a_shell_is_mounted: body:has(.t-app-shell) { margin: 0 } — a deliberate global rule, scoped so it only applies to pages that mount a shell

responsive_measured_on_root_via_resize_observer: the shell root's own width drives the policy, not the viewport or content panel; a component `as` (rather than an element) degrades gracefully to no auto-switching

three_state_rail_policy_when_rail_breakpoint_set: drawer < breakpoint <= rail < railBreakpoint <= expanded; the auto-rail band forces the collapsed rail as the effective state without changing the manual preference or emitting collapse events
desktop_sidebar_is_removed_from_the_dom_on_mobile: crossing the breakpoint unmounts and remounts the sidebar slot, so component state inside it is not preserved

immersive_hides_chrome_without_remounting_content: the header and sidebar are hidden with CSS and the grid collapses to a single `main` area, so the default slot keeps its component state across the toggle — unlike the mobile breakpoint, which does remount the sidebar slot
immersive_suppresses_the_mobile_drawer: while immersive the overlay is not rendered, since the trigger that opens it lives in the hidden header
controlled_mode_does_not_self_update: when collapsed, sidebarOpen or immersive is bound, the component only emits — the parent must apply the value
root_data_attribute: data-mobile="true" | "false" on the shell root

useWhen: You need a full-viewport application layout with a header, a side navigation, and a fluid content area.
The layout should use the entire page width (not a centered max-width) and switch the sidebar to an off-canvas drawer on small screens.
avoidWhen: You only need one region (just a top bar, or just a side rail) — compose TNavbar or TSidebar directly.
The page content should be centered with a readable max width — use TContainer inside the shell content instead.

alternatives.when: You want a standalone collapsible side rail without the header/content orchestration or responsive drawer.
You only need the top application bar.
You need a general temporary overlay panel rather than the shell's responsive navigation.

notes: Mount at the app root; it fills the viewport (100dvh, full width) and scrolls its content region independently.
Put navigation in the `sidebar` slot (e.g. `TNavMenu`) — it inherits collapsed state from the shell provider like `TSidebar`.
Leave `mobile` unset to auto-switch to an off-canvas drawer at `breakpoint`, or bind `mobile` to control it explicitly.
Use `v-model:collapsed` for the desktop rail and `v-model:sidebarOpen` for the mobile drawer when the app persists layout state.

components.name: TAvatar
components.description: User avatar that renders an image when `src` is set, otherwise initials derived from `alt`, plus an optional status indicator dot.

props.name: src
props.description: URL of the avatar image. When set, the `` branch renders and the initials span is NOT in the DOM at all (`v-else`). If the image fails to load, the `` is hidden via inline `display: none` — the component does NOT fall back to initials, leaving an empty circle. Pass no `src` if you want initials.

props.name: alt
props.description: Accessible label for the avatar and the `alt` attribute of the ``. Also the source for derived initials when `initials` is not set. Defaults to an empty string, in which case no `aria-label` is rendered at all (`alt || undefined`) and derived initials are empty — always pass a meaningful `alt`.

props.name: initials
props.description: Explicit initials, rendered only when `src` is unset. Takes precedence over initials derived from `alt`. When omitted, initials are derived from `alt` by splitting on spaces, taking the first character of the first two words, and uppercasing (e.g. "Jane Doe" -> "JD").

props.name: size
props.description: Fixed square dimensions — sm 2rem, md 2.5rem, lg 3.5rem — with matching font size for initials.

props.name: status
props.description: Shows a colored status dot pinned to the bottom-right of the avatar bounding box. Renders alongside either branch (image or initials). The dot sits on the corner outside the circular mask, so `.t-avatar` deliberately has no `overflow: hidden`.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Optional overlay content, rendered inside the `.t-avatar` span after the image/initials and before the status dot. The parent is `position: relative`, so children can be absolutely positioned. Note the wrapper carries `role="img"`, which makes its subtree presentational to assistive technology — slot content is visual only and will not be announced.

a11y: role: img (always set on the root span, including when only initials render)
aria-label: Set from `alt`. Omitted entirely when `alt` is empty (the default), which leaves the `role="img"` element without an accessible name.
initials: Marked `aria-hidden="true"` — the name comes from `alt`, not the visible text.
image: Receives the `alt` prop as its native `alt` attribute.
status_dot: Carries `aria-label` with the status value, but the element is a bare `<span>` with no role, so the label is not exposed to assistive technology. Convey status in text if it matters.
slot_content: Not announced — the `role="img"` root makes descendant content presentational.

components.name: TBadge
components.purpose: Compact status or metadata label with optional icon.

props.name: variant
props.description: Surface treatment. Selects which --tree-badge-<variant>-* colour variables the badge consumes; danger is a surface style, not a semantic guarantee — its red palette is replaced when tone is success, warning, or info.

props.name: tone
props.description: Semantic palette. Re-maps the --tree-badge-* colour variables that the chosen variant consumes, so it recolours the badge without changing its surface treatment. neutral keeps the brand palette.

props.name: size
props.description: Changes min-height and font-size only; inline padding is constant across sizes.

a11y: tone_conveys_meaning_by_color_only: No role or aria state is set, so put the status word in the badge text rather than relying on tone alone.

components.name: TBrandLockup
components.purpose: Generic product brand lockup — a logo mark plus a title and optional subtitle, aligned and collapse-aware, with an optional home link. Owns sizing, truncation, collapse and link so the shell header does not hand-roll them. Ships NO identity: the logo asset and the strings belong to the consumer.

props.name: title
props.note: Product name. Rendered via TText with truncation.

props.name: subtitle
props.note: Optional supporting line under the title. Muted, smaller, truncates.

props.name: size
props.note: Scales the logo height (sm → icon-lg, md → icon-xl, lg → control-md) and the title size.

props.name: collapsed
props.note: Hide the text, keeping only the logo — for a shell's collapsed rail. Bind it to TAppShell's scoped `collapsed` slot prop. When collapsed AND used as a link, the accessible name must come from the logo (e.g. `alt` on the ``), since the visible text is gone.

props.name: href
props.note: Native href. Renders the lockup as an `<a>` (home link).

props.name: to
props.note: Route target. Renders as RouterLink when vue-router is available, else falls back to `<a>`. Without href/to the root is a plain `<div>`.

slots.name: logo
slots.description: The logo mark — an `` or inline `` an `alt` for the accessible name.

a11y: logo_not_cropped: The logo keeps its true proportions; a non-square wordmark is not distorted.
link_accessible_name: As a link, the name comes from the title text, or from the logo `alt` when collapsed.
focus_visible: Focus ring on the anchor when it is a link.

components.name: TBreadcrumb
components.purpose: Hierarchical navigation trail showing the user's current location within a site or application.

props.name: separator
props.description: Character or string shown between items. Applied as the inline CSS custom property --tree-breadcrumb-separator on the <ol>, wrapped in single quotes, and drawn by the .t-breadcrumb__item:not(:last-child)::after pseudo-element — it never enters the DOM as text. A value containing a single quote or a backslash produces an invalid CSS value and the separator stops rendering.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Breadcrumb trail content, rendered inside the <ol class='t-breadcrumb__list'>. Expects TBreadcrumbItem children so each entry becomes an <li>. No scoped props.

behavior: link_target_precedence: to + resolvable RouterLink, then href, then string to

notes: Use `href` for native links.
Use `to` when the app has vue-router and breadcrumb items should render RouterLink.

components.name: TButton
components.purpose: Clickable action trigger with variants, sizes, optional polymorphism, and loading state.

props.name: as
props.note: Root tag rendered through `<component :is>`. Any value other than `button` switches the component onto the non-native accessibility path (see `a11y`) and stops the `type` attribute from being rendered.

props.name: variant
props.note: `brand` is a button-only, high-emphasis gradient variant driven by the `--tree-gradient-brand` token. Use it for the single primary CTA of a screen.

props.name: size
props.note: `t-button--md` is emitted but carries no CSS rule of its own — md is the base sizing, and only sm/lg override height, padding, and font size.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Applied as the native `disabled` attribute only when `as` is `button`; for any other tag the component falls back to `aria-disabled="true"` plus `tabindex="-1"`. Clicks are blocked in both cases.

props.name: loading
props.note: loading implies disabled behavior — the effective disabled state is `disabled || loading`, so `loading` on its own disables the button and applies `is-disabled`.

props.name: loadingLabel
props.note: Accessible announcement while loading, forwarded to the nested TSpinner's `label`. The default is English because TreeUI ships no i18n layer — pass the active locale's string to localize it (see DECISIONS.md → Locale Data).

props.name: hideIconWhileLoading
props.note: While loading, the `icon` slot is not rendered so the spinner replaces the icon instead of rendering beside it. Set to `false` to restore the pre-0.19 behavior of showing both.

props.name: iconOnly
props.note: Square, icon-only button — collapses the inline padding to a 1:1 control matching the size token and suppresses the `t-button__label` wrapper. Requires an accessible name via `label` (or `aria-label`), since the icon slot is aria-hidden.

props.name: label
props.note: Accessible name, rendered as `aria-label`. Required whenever `iconOnly` is set; ignored for ordinary buttons that already have visible text. In development, an `iconOnly` button with no `label`/`aria-label`/`aria-labelledby` logs a console warning; the check is guarded by `process.env.NODE_ENV` so it is stripped from the consumer's production build (the vue package's vite build keeps `process.env.NODE_ENV` as a runtime reference for this).

props.name: type
props.note: Only forwarded when `as` is `button`; for any other tag no `type` attribute is rendered. Set `type="submit"` for the submit control of an enclosing form.

props.name: block
props.note: Stretches the button to the full width of its container. Useful for menu rows and mobile CTAs.

props.name: align
props.note: Content alignment; only meaningful once the button is wider than its content, which in practice means together with `block`, since the base rule is `min-width: max-content`. Use `start` for left-aligned icon+label menu rows. The default `center` emits no modifier class — it is the base rule.

events.name: click
events.description: Emitted on activation. Suppressed while disabled or loading: the internal handler calls preventDefault() and stopPropagation() and returns without emitting.

a11y: loading_spinner_is_announced: The nested TSpinner carries role="status" and a visually hidden label, so the button's accessible name gains that text while loading. The text comes from `loadingLabel`, so it follows the app's locale.

icon_only_requires_an_accessible_name: With `iconOnly` there is no visible text and the icon slot is aria-hidden, so the name must come from `label` or `aria-label`. This is a documented requirement, not a runtime assertion — the package ships ESM+CJS with no dev-only build define, so there is no dev-stripped warning.
focus_visible: box-shadow focus ring on the root element
behavior: spinner_replaces_the_icon_slot_while_loading: The spinner takes the icon's leading position, so by default the icon slot is withheld while loading and the order is spinner, label. Set `hideIconWhileLoading: false` for the older spinner, icon, label order.

components.name: TCard
components.purpose: Surface container with optional header, actions, and footer slots.

props.name: as
props.description: Tag or component used for the polymorphic root, rendered through `<component :is="as">`. Change it to `article`, `aside`, `li`, or `div` when the default `section` element is wrong for the surrounding document structure. It only swaps the element — classes, slots, and layout are unchanged.

props.name: size
props.description: Padding scale only — it does not change typography, border, or radius. `sm` uses --tree-space-3, `md` --tree-space-4, `lg` --tree-space-5.

props.name: title
props.description: Optional card heading. Rendered as the fallback content of the `header` slot inside a `span.t-card__title`, so supplying `#header` replaces it entirely. Setting it also forces the header element to render. Because it is declared as a prop it is consumed by the component and never reaches the root element as the native HTML `title` tooltip attribute.

props.name: interactive
props.description: Treats the card as an interactive surface — adds hover elevation, a focus-visible ring, `cursor: pointer`, and clears text-decoration/colour. Use with `as="a"` / `as="router-link"` to make the whole card a single link WITHOUT an inner TLink, which would underline the card's text via decoration inheritance. The consumer owns the interactive element's accessible name and href/to; the card only supplies the affordance.

slots.name: header
slots.description: Replaces the `title` fallback inside `header.t-card__header`. When this slot is supplied the `title` prop renders nothing. Receives no slot props.

slots.name: actions
slots.description: Trailing controls wrapped in `span.t-card__actions`, rendered as a sibling after the header content inside the same `header.t-card__header`. Supplying it is enough to render the header on its own, with no `title` and no `header` slot. Receives no slot props.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Card body. Always rendered, wrapped in `div.t-card__body`. Receives no slot props.

slots.name: footer
slots.description: Renders `footer.t-card__footer` only when the slot is supplied; there is no `footer` prop fallback. Receives no slot props.

a11y: root_role: None applied by the component — semantics come entirely from `as` (default `section`).

title_is_not_a_heading: `title` renders a `span.t-card__title`, styled at heading size and weight but carrying no heading semantics, so it does not enter the document outline. When the card must be reachable by heading navigation, put a real `<h2>`/`<h3>` in the `header` slot instead of using the prop.

unnamed_section_root: The default `section` root has no accessible name, so assistive tech exposes it as generic rather than as a region. The component sets no `inheritAttrs: false` and has a single root, so `aria-label` / `aria-labelledby` passed by the consumer fall through to that root when a named region is wanted.
behavior: header_render_condition: The `header.t-card__header` element renders when ANY of the `header` slot, a truthy `title` prop, or the `actions` slot is present. All three branches are independent — actions alone still produces a header.
footer_render_condition: The `footer.t-card__footer` element renders only when the `footer` slot is supplied.
slot_props: None. No slot on this component is scoped; all four are plain content slots.

variant_effects: {"outline":"Base surface with a default border. The styling baseline — the `t-card--outline` class is emitted but carries no CSS rule of its own.","soft":"Subtle tinted surface (bg-subtle), same border and shadow as outline.","inset":"Same tinted surface as soft, but with no shadow, so it reads as recessed inside another surface."}
note: No card variant inverts its surface, so nested content inherits the normal color tokens. For a high-emphasis card, use a brand-colored border rather than an inverted background.

useWhen: You need a framed surface for related content.
The layout can be composed from slots instead of a dedicated domain component.
avoidWhen: You only need width constraint or spacing.

alternatives.when: The main need is width constraint.
The main need is spacing between children.

components.name: TChart
components.purpose: Lightweight cartesian chart (line, area, bar) for multi-series data, with axes, gridlines, hover tooltip, legend, and an accessible data table. No external charting dependency.

props.name: series
props.note: Each item is { name: string; data: number[]; color?: string }. Series with an empty data array are dropped before rendering — they never reach the plot, legend, tooltip, or a11y table.

props.name: labels
props.note: X-axis categories, aligned index-for-index to each series' data. Labels past the longest series are ignored; a missing label falls back to '#<n>' in the tooltip and to an empty header cell in the a11y table.

props.name: height
props.note: Width is fluid (measured via ResizeObserver).

props.name: smooth
props.note: Curved line/area; ignored for bar.

props.name: stacked
props.note: Stacks bar series; ignored for line/area. Negative values are clamped to 0 in stacked mode (grouped mode renders them below the baseline). The tooltip always reports the raw per-series values, not the cumulative stack.

props.name: showGrid
props.note: Horizontal gridlines at the y ticks. Independent of showYAxis, which controls the tick text.

props.name: showLegend
props.default: auto (true when more than one series)
props.note: The legend is suppressed whenever there is no data, even when set to true.

props.name: showXAxis
props.note: When false the category labels are skipped and the bottom margin collapses from 28px to 10px.

props.name: showYAxis
props.note: When false the tick text is skipped and the left margin collapses from 44px to 10px. Gridlines are unaffected.

props.name: showTooltip
props.note: Gates hover tracking as a whole: the crosshair, the tooltip panel, and the point-click event all stop when false.

props.name: showPoints
props.note: Always draw markers on line/area points; ignored for bar.

props.name: yTicks
props.note: Target tick count handed to niceScale — the rendered count may differ once the bounds are rounded.

props.name: valueFormat
props.note: Applies to y-axis ticks, tooltip values, and the a11y table cells.

props.name: minY
props.default: auto (min(0, data min) for bar/area, data min for line)
props.note: A hint, not a hard bound: niceScale rounds the low end down, so the rendered domain can start below it.

props.name: maxY
props.note: A hint, not a hard bound: niceScale rounds the high end up, so the rendered domain can extend above it.

props.name: animated
props.note: Draw-in animation via the is-animated root class; disabled under prefers-reduced-motion.

props.name: loading
props.note: Replaces the plot with five pulsing skeleton bars and adds the is-loading root class. Takes precedence over the empty state; the legend still renders when data is present.

props.name: ariaLabel
props.note: Rendered as the <caption> of the visually-hidden data table — it is not applied as an aria-label attribute on the root.

events.name: point-click
events.payload: { index: number; label: string; values: { name: string; value: number; color: string }[] }

a11y: SVG is aria-hidden; a visually-hidden <table> mirrors the series data for assistive tech.
The table's <caption> carries ariaLabel (or the auto-generated summary); the root div itself has no role or accessible name.
Legend defaults on for multiple series so identity is not color-only.
Provide a meaningful ariaLabel describing what the chart shows.
point-click has no keyboard equivalent — do not make it the only way to reach a behavior.
behavior: colors_auto_assign_from_chart_palette: --tree-color-chart-1..8, cycling by series index; per-series color overrides
zero_baseline_for_bar_and_area: the y domain is extended to include 0 for bar and area; line uses the data extent. minY overrides it.
y_domain_uses_nice_ticks: every type runs through niceScale, which rounds the min down and the max up to round numbers
hover_shows_crosshair_and_tooltip: only while showTooltip is true
width_measured_with_resize_observer: falls back to 640px until the canvas is mounted and measured
x_labels_decimate_when_crowded: keeps every max(2, floor(innerWidth / 52))-th label and hides the rest; the last category is always kept

useWhen: You need a line, area, or bar chart with axes, gridlines, legend, and a hover tooltip.
One or more series share a set of category labels.
avoidWhen: A single value tells the story — use TStat.
You only need an inline trend with no axes — use TSparkline.
The data is a part-of-whole breakdown — use TDonutChart.

alternatives.when: The trend should be inline and axis-less.
The data is proportional (parts of a whole).

notes: Pass `series` as `{ name, data, color? }[]` and `labels` as a parallel `string[]` (one label per data index).
Set `type` to `line`, `area`, or `bar`; use `stacked` only with `bar`.
Series colors come from `--tree-color-chart-1..8` in order; pass `color` per series to override — do not remap colors when the series set changes.
Pass `valueFormat: (n) => string` to format axis and tooltip values (currency, compact, etc.).
No charting library or provider is required — the SVG and geometry are built in.

components.name: TCheckbox
components.purpose: Boolean toggle with indeterminate state; supports v-model via modelValue.

props.name: modelValue
props.description: Checked state. Fully controlled — the component holds no internal state and never mutates this prop; it emits update:modelValue and the parent must write the value back.

props.name: size
props.description: Control and label scale. Always rendered as a t-checkbox--<size> modifier.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Sets the native disabled attribute on the input and adds is-disabled to the root. No aria-disabled is emitted — the native attribute carries the state.

props.name: indeterminate
props.description: Mixed state. Renders the dash icon and sets aria-checked="mixed", taking precedence over modelValue for both. Synced to the input's native indeterminate DOM property.

props.name: invalid
props.description: Adds is-invalid to the root and aria-invalid="true" to the input. When false the aria-invalid attribute is omitted entirely, not set to "false".

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted on the native input's change event. The payload is always the negation of the current modelValue prop, not the DOM checked state. Enables v-model binding.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Label content, wrapped in <span class="t-checkbox__label">. Rendered only when the slot is supplied (v-if="$slots.default"). The root element is a <label> that wraps the input, so slot text labels the control implicitly — no for/id pairing is needed.

a11y: accessible_name: From the default slot; absent when the slot is omitted — supply aria-label or aria-labelledby
aria_checked_explicitly_bound: 'mixed' when indeterminate, otherwise the modelValue boolean
input_is_visually_hidden_but_focusable: clip-rect hidden input keeps native semantics and tab order; the focus ring is drawn on t-checkbox__control
behavior: controlled_only: No internal state; the checkbox does not change appearance until the parent updates modelValue
indeterminate_precedence: indeterminate wins over modelValue for the rendered icon and for aria-checked; is-checked and is-indeterminate can both be present at once
indeterminate_is_parent_owned: The component never clears indeterminate — set it to false yourself when handling update:modelValue

useWhen: Each option is independent.
You need standard checkbox form semantics.
avoidWhen: Exactly one option must be selected.

alternatives.when: Only one option is valid.
The choice is a single immediate on/off state.

components.name: TCodeBlock
components.purpose: Scrollable monospace surface for logs, JSON, ARNs and other machine text. Block-level only — for inline code/IDs use `TText family="mono"`. No syntax highlighting and no virtualization by design; assumes bounded content (e.g. a limited log tail).

props.name: code
props.note: Code as a string. Rendered as the block content and required for the built-in copy button. Omit and use the default slot for pre-formatted markup.

props.name: wrap
props.note: `false` keeps lines intact and scrolls horizontally (right for logs). `true` wraps long lines (`pre-wrap` + `overflow-wrap`, right for JSON).

props.name: maxBlockSize
props.note: Any CSS length. Caps the height; the block scrolls vertically beyond it.

props.name: copyable
props.note: Show the built-in copy button. Requires `code` (there is nothing to copy from a slot). Copies via navigator.clipboard and emits `copy`.

props.name: label
props.note: Accessible name for the scrollable region and the copy button. English default, overridable (TreeUI ships no i18n layer).

events.name: copy
events.description: Emitted after the built-in copy button writes `code` to the clipboard.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Block content when `code` is not used. Rendered inside `<pre><code>`. The built-in copy button is not shown for slot content (nothing to copy from).

slots.name: actions
slots.description: Reserved for extra toolbar controls.

a11y: scroll_region: The `<pre>` is `role="region"` + `tabindex="0"` + `aria-label` (from `label`) so keyboard users can scroll it — the library does this for you.
copy_button_state: The copy button's aria-label reflects the copied state briefly.

components.name: TColorSwatch
components.purpose: Pick a colour from a row of preset swatches, with an optional native colour input for an arbitrary value. Built for accent/brand pickers.

props.name: modelValue
props.note: Selected colour as a hex string. Matched against `options[].value` by case-insensitive exact string comparison, so `#0969DA` matches `#0969da` but `#fff` never matches `#ffffff`. A value matching no preset leaves every swatch unselected and is still written to the custom input.

props.name: options
props.note: Preset swatches, each `{ label, value }`. `value` is applied raw as the button `background`, so any CSS colour renders, but keep it `#rrggbb` so matching and the custom input agree. `label` is the button's only accessible name — the button renders no text content. An empty array renders an empty group (only the custom input, when enabled).

props.name: allowCustom
props.note: Appends a native `<input type="color">` after the presets for values outside them. When false the input is not rendered at all and `customLabel` has no effect.

props.name: size
props.note: Drives `--tree-color-swatch-size` (sm 1.5rem, md 1.75rem, lg 2rem). Swatches are square; the custom input is one `--tree-space-2` wider.

props.name: label
props.note: Accessible label for the swatch group (`role="group"`). Rendered as `aria-label` on the root, so an `aria-label` passed by the consumer falls through to the same element and overrides it.

props.name: customLabel
props.note: Accessible label (`aria-label`) for the native colour input, and its only accessible name. Applies only when `allowCustom` is set.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Sets the native `disabled` attribute on every preset button and on the custom input, so the group stops emitting and drops out of the Tab order. Not reflected on the wrapper, and there is no `is-disabled` class.

a11y: Each swatch is a native `<button type="button">` with `aria-pressed` reflecting selection and `aria-label` from the option label; it has no text content, so an option without a `label` is unnamed.
Selection is conveyed by an outline (not colour alone), since the swatch's own colour is the content.
The custom input is a native `<input type="color">` named only by `customLabel`.
No roving focus and no arrow-key navigation — every swatch and the custom input is its own Tab stop.

components.name: TCombobox
components.purpose: Searchable combobox / autocomplete input with local filtering, controlled selection, and shared open-state behavior.

props.name: modelValue
props.note: The SELECTED option value, never the text in the input. The input renders an internal `query` string that is re-synced from the selected option's label whenever the listbox is closed. A `modelValue` with no matching entry in `options` resolves to an empty label.

props.name: options
props.note: Array of { label: string; value: string; description?: string; keywords?: string[]; disabled?: boolean }

props.name: open
props.note: Controlled open state. Once a boolean is passed the component stops tracking open state internally — it only emits update:open / open-change and the consumer MUST write the value back, or the listbox never opens.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.note: Uncontrolled initial open state, read once on mount. Later changes to it are ignored.

props.name: size
props.note: Sizes the control only. `md` has no CSS rule of its own — it is the base `.t-combobox__control` styling.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Sets the native `disabled` attribute on the input, adds `is-disabled` to the root, and suppresses the listbox entirely (`v-if="isOpen && !disabled"`) — even while `open` is true. Also short-circuits opening, typing side effects, and keydown handling.

props.name: loading
props.note: Presentational only. Replaces the chevron indicator with `<TSpinner size="sm" label="Loading" />` and sets aria-busy; it never blocks typing, opening, filtering, or selection.

props.name: emptyText
props.note: Fallback content of the `empty` slot — supplying that slot shadows this prop entirely.

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted with the chosen option's `value` when an option is selected, and with `""` when the listbox closes while the input text is blank and a value was set (the clear-by-erasing path).

events.name: update:open
events.description: Emitted whenever the open state changes. Deduped — no emit when the next value equals the current one.

events.name: open-change
events.description: Emitted immediately after `update:open`, with the same payload. Alias for consumers that do not use v-model:open.

events.name: input-change
events.description: Emitted on every keystroke with the raw, untrimmed input text — the same string the `empty` slot receives as `query`. This is the only hook for server-side / async filtering; it does not replace or disable the built-in local filtering, so an async consumer must feed results back through `options`.

slots.name: prefix
slots.description: Leading content inside `.t-combobox__control`, before the input. Rendered only when the slot is supplied, wrapped in `<span class="t-combobox__slot t-combobox__slot--prefix">`. No scoped props.

slots.name: option
slots.description: Replaces the default option body (label, description, and the selected check icon). Renders INSIDE the row's `<li role="option">`, which owns the click and mouseenter handlers, so it must contain only presentational content. `selected` is `option.value === modelValue`; `active` is the keyboard-highlighted row, which is NOT DOM focus — focus stays on the input at all times.

slots.name: empty
slots.description: Replaces `emptyText` inside `<li class="t-combobox__empty">` when the filter matches no option. `query` is the current raw input text, untrimmed and uncased. Passing both this slot and `emptyText` renders only the slot.

a11y: accessible_name: None is generated — there is no label element and no default slot. Pass aria-label or aria-labelledby (which also names the listbox), or pass `id` and wrap in TFormField with a matching `html-for`; TFormField does not link the label on its own.

useWhen: The user needs to filter or search a single-choice list.
A dropdown is appropriate, but plain select is too limited.
avoidWhen: Search is unnecessary and the option list is short.

alternatives.when: You only need a standard dropdown without filtering.
The user must select multiple values.

notes: Pass `options` as labeled items and keep labels human-readable because search matches visible fields.

components.name: TConfirmDialog
components.purpose: High-level confirmation dialog built on the shared modal contract, with default confirm and cancel actions.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.note: Uncontrolled initial state. Read once on setup — later changes are ignored. Only meaningful when `open` is left undefined.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Only blocks opening from the `trigger` slot (the trigger wrapper gets `aria-disabled` and its click is swallowed). It does NOT block a programmatic open through `open`/`defaultOpen`, and it does not disable the confirm/cancel buttons — use `confirmDisabled`/`cancelDisabled` for those.

props.name: size
props.note: Forwarded to TModal and applied as `t-modal__surface--<size>` — it sizes the dialog surface, not the confirm-dialog block, and it does not size the default footer buttons (those are always TButton `md`).

props.name: title
props.note: Rendered by TModal as `t-modal__title` and referenced by `aria-labelledby`. TConfirmDialog does not expose TModal's `header` slot, so an empty title leaves the dialog without an accessible name.

props.name: description
props.note: Rendered by TModal as `t-modal__description` and referenced by `aria-describedby`. The default slot body is NOT part of the accessible description.

props.name: confirmLabel
props.note: Ignored when the `actions` slot is supplied.

props.name: cancelLabel
props.note: Ignored when the `actions` slot is supplied.

props.name: confirmVariant
props.note: Ignored when the `actions` slot is supplied.

props.name: confirmDisabled
props.note: Disables the default confirm button AND short-circuits the confirm handler — no `confirm` emit and no close, including when the handler is invoked from the `actions` slot scope.

props.name: cancelDisabled
props.note: Disables the default cancel button AND short-circuits the cancel handler — no `cancel` emit and no close, including when the handler is invoked from the `actions` slot scope.

props.name: loading
props.note: Puts the default confirm button into its spinner state (TButton adds `is-loading`, `aria-busy`, and the native `disabled` attribute) and short-circuits the confirm handler exactly like `confirmDisabled`. It does not affect the cancel button. Also passed to the `actions` slot scope so a custom footer can mirror the state.

props.name: closeOnOverlay
props.note: Deliberately opt-in — TModal defaults this to true, TConfirmDialog flips it to false so a confirmation is not dismissed by a stray backdrop click.

props.name: showCloseButton
props.note: Deliberately opt-in — TModal defaults this to true. Enabling it adds a header close button that dismisses without emitting `cancel`.

props.name: closeLabel
props.note: Accessible name of the header close button. Only rendered when `showCloseButton` is true.

props.name: closeOnConfirm
props.note: Set false for async confirmation: `confirm` fires but the dialog stays open, so the caller can hold it open with `loading` while the request resolves and then close it via `open`.

props.name: closeOnCancel
props.note: Set false to keep the dialog open after `cancel` — the caller then owns closing via `open`.

slots.name: trigger
slots.description: Element that opens the dialog on click. Omit for imperative use.

slots.name: icon
slots.description: Decorative leading icon. Presence switches the block to the two-column icon layout.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Extra body content rendered under the description.

slots.name: actions
slots.description: Replaces the whole footer. Scope gives you confirm(), cancel(), and loading.

a11y: restore_focus_caveat: TModal only records the previously focused element when the dialog is opened by clicking its `trigger` slot. A dialog opened programmatically — by setting `open` from a row action, a menu item, or `defaultOpen` — has nothing recorded, so focus is not restored on close. Move focus back yourself in that case.
accessible_name_source: `title` prop, via aria-labelledby; absent when `title` is empty
accessible_description_source: `description` prop, via aria-describedby; the default slot body is not included

initial_focus: First focusable element inside the dialog surface. With the default footer and `showCloseButton: false` that is the cancel button, which is the safe landing spot for a destructive confirmation. Enabling `showCloseButton` moves initial focus to the header close button instead. A `loading` or `confirmDisabled` confirm button carries the native `disabled` attribute and is skipped.
behavior: confirm_suppressed_when: confirmDisabled || loading — the handler returns before emitting, so neither `confirm` nor the close runs
cancel_suppressed_when: cancelDisabled — the handler returns before emitting, so neither `cancel` nor the close runs

components.name: TContainer
components.purpose: Width-constrained layout wrapper for readable page content and app sections.

props.name: padded
props.note: Adds `.is-padded`, i.e. an inline padding of `clamp(var(--tree-space-4), 3vw, var(--tree-space-6))`.

props.name: centered
props.note: Adds `.is-centered`, i.e. `margin-inline: auto`.

a11y: Acts as a structural wrapper only; semantics come from the `as` prop and child content.

Renders a non-semantic `div` by default. Pass `as` to make it a landmark (e.g. `as="main"`, `as="section"`, `as="header"`) — avoid nesting a second `main` inside an app shell or a TPage that already provides one.
`aria-*` and `role` attributes fall through to the root element (see `forwards_attrs_to`).

useWhen: You want consistent max width and horizontal page padding.
The page shell should stay full-width while content stays readable.
avoidWhen: You need internal spacing between children.
You are laying out a whole screen — reach for TPage, which adds page padding and vertical rhythm.

alternatives.when: You want a full page region with block padding and vertical spacing between sections, not just a width constraint.
The main need is vertical or horizontal spacing.
The main need is responsive columns.

components.name: TContextMenu
components.description: Right-click (contextmenu) menu teleported to body and positioned at the cursor. The component renders no chrome of its own — the root wrapper is `display: contents` and only wraps the slotted trigger area.

props.name: items
props.description: Menu items to render, one `role="menuitem"` <li> each. An empty array is allowed and still opens an empty `role="menu"` list with nothing focused.

props.name: open
props.description: Controlled open state (v-model:open). When defined it wins over the internal state and `defaultOpen` is ignored; the component still emits and expects the parent to write the new value back. `disabled` overrides this — a disabled component renders no menu even while `open` is true.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.description: Uncontrolled initial open state. Read once during setup — later changes to this prop have no effect.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Prevents the context menu from opening, and hides it if it is already open. While disabled the component does not call preventDefault on `contextmenu`, so the native browser menu appears instead.

props.name: size
props.description: Item sizing variant. The modifier class is applied to both the root and the teleported menu, but only the copy on the menu is effective (the root is teleport-detached from the menu subtree).

events.name: select
events.description: Emitted with `item.value` when an enabled item is activated by click or Enter/Space. Disabled items emit nothing.

events.name: update:open
events.description: Emitted when open state changes (v-model support). Only fires on an actual transition — setting the same value again is a no-op.

events.name: open-change
events.description: Emitted when open state changes. Always fires together with `update:open`, including in controlled mode.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Content that acts as the right-click trigger area. It is rendered inside the root <div>, which is `display: contents` and therefore contributes no box of its own — the slotted content keeps its own layout, and the `contextmenu` handler still fires through event bubbling.

slots.name: item
slots.description: Custom rendering for each menu item; scoped props `{ item, index }`, falls back to `{{ item.label }}`. Rendered inside the `role="menuitem"` <li>, which already owns click, keydown, tabindex and aria-disabled — keep the content presentational, nested interactive controls are not reachable inside a menuitem.

a11y: role: menu (on the teleported floating list)

keyboard: ["Right-click / contextmenu event opens menu at cursor","ArrowDown / ArrowUp move focus to the next / previous enabled item (clamped, no wrap)","Home / End jumps to first / last enabled item","Enter / Space selects focused item","Escape closes menu and restores focus","All key handling lives on the items — keys are only handled while an item has focus"]

aria: ["aria-disabled on disabled items (omitted, not false, when enabled)","aria-label=\"Context menu\" on the menu element — hardcoded, not configurable or localizable","Menu id is generated with createId('t-context-menu'); there is no `id` prop","tabindex 0 on enabled items, -1 on disabled items — no roving tabindex, every enabled item is tab-reachable"]

focus: ["First enabled item receives focus on open","When every item is disabled, focus still goes to the first item; with an empty `items` array nothing is focused","Focus restored to trigger area on close from Escape or item selection","An outside click closes without restoring focus, leaving it where the pointer put it"]
behavior: Opens at cursor position on right-click (contextmenu event), using event.clientX / clientY.
Uses Teleport to body to escape stacking contexts; the menu is not a DOM descendant of the trigger.
Uses position fixed at the raw cursor coordinates, stacked at var(--tree-z-dropdown).
No collision detection or viewport clamping — a menu opened near the right or bottom edge overflows.
Closes on pointerdown outside the menu (including on the trigger itself), Escape, or item selection.
