Right-clicking again inside the trigger area repositions the menu — the outside pointerdown closes it and the following contextmenu reopens it at the new coordinates.
A document-level contextmenu listener covers keyboard-invoked menus (Shift+F10 / Menu key) raised outside the trigger.
Prevents native browser context menu on trigger area, but only while not disabled.
Exposes data-state="open" | "closed" on the root element for external styling.
Document pointerdown / contextmenu listeners are attached only while open, and removed on close and before unmount.
Menu enter / leave uses the shared t-fade transition (opacity plus scale 0.95).

useWhen: Actions are triggered from context-click or long-press style behavior.
avoidWhen: The menu is triggered from a standard button.

alternatives.when: A normal trigger button opens the menu.

components.name: TDatePicker
components.purpose: Date input with calendar popover and keyboard navigation.

props.name: modelValue
props.note: Parsed with `parseDateValue`, which accepts ONLY a strict `YYYY-MM-DD` string that resolves to a real calendar date. Anything else (empty, `2026-13-01`, an ISO datetime, a Date object stringified) resolves to null and the trigger falls back to `placeholder`. The hidden input, when present, still submits the raw prop verbatim — an unparseable value renders as the placeholder but is submitted as-is.

props.name: open
props.note: Controlled open state. Once a boolean is passed the component stops tracking open state internally — it only emits update:open / open-change and the consumer MUST write the value back, or the calendar never opens.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.note: Uncontrolled initial open state, read once on mount. Later changes to it are ignored. Opening this way skips `openCalendar`, so no day button receives DOM focus until the user tabs or arrows into the grid.

props.name: size
props.note: Sizes the trigger only, never the popover. `md` has no CSS rule of its own — it is the base `.t-date-picker__trigger` styling — but the `t-date-picker--md` class is still rendered.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Sets the native `disabled` attribute on the trigger, adds `is-disabled` to the root, short-circuits `openCalendar` and trigger keydown, makes `isDateDisabled` return true for every date, and suppresses the popover entirely (`v-if="isOpen && !disabled"`) even while `open` is true.

props.name: invalid
props.note: Adds `is-invalid` to the root and `aria-invalid="true"` to the trigger. Purely presentational — it does not block opening or selection.

props.name: placeholder
props.note: Rendered inside `.t-date-picker__value` with a `data-placeholder` attribute whenever `modelValue` does not parse.

props.name: locale
props.note: BCP-47 tag passed to three separate `Intl.DateTimeFormat` calls: the trigger text (day numeric / month long / year numeric), the popover month caption (month long / year numeric), and the weekday header (weekday short). It does NOT localize the per-day `aria-label`, which is always the raw `YYYY-MM-DD` key.

props.name: min
props.note: Inclusive lower bound. Days before it get the native `disabled` attribute and cannot be selected or arrowed onto; the Previous-month nav button is disabled once the view reaches the min month. A value that fails `parseDateValue` is ignored, i.e. treated as no bound.

props.name: max
props.note: Inclusive upper bound, mirroring `min`: out-of-range days are disabled and the Next-month nav button is disabled once the view reaches the max month.

props.name: weekStartsOn
props.note: 0 = Sunday. Drives the weekday header order, the leading offset of the day grid, and the Home/End keys (which jump to the first/last day of the week as defined by this prop).

props.name: id
props.note: Names the CALENDAR POPOVER, not the trigger. It becomes the popover's `id` (referenced by the trigger's `aria-controls` while open) and seeds the month caption id as `${id}-caption`. Read once at setup — later changes are ignored. Because `id` is a declared prop it never falls through to the trigger button, so the trigger has no id and a `<label for>` / `TFormField html-for` cannot target it; name the control with `aria-label` instead.

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted with the chosen day as `YYYY-MM-DD` from `selectDate` only — a click or Enter/Space on an enabled day button. Nothing else in the component emits it; there is no clear affordance, so the value never becomes `""` on its own.

events.name: update:open
events.description: Emitted whenever the open state changes. Deduped — no emit when the next value equals the current one.

events.name: open-change
events.description: Emitted immediately after `update:open`, with the same payload. Alias for consumers that do not use v-model:open.

events.name: select
events.description: Emitted from `selectDate` immediately after `update:modelValue`, with the same `YYYY-MM-DD` payload. It is the user-intent signal: it fires only on an actual day activation and never when `modelValue` changes from outside, which is what distinguishes it from `update:modelValue`.

slots.name: prefix
slots.description: Leading content inside the trigger button, before the value text. Rendered only when the slot is supplied, wrapped in `<span class="t-date-picker__slot t-date-picker__slot--prefix">`. No scoped props. Content is inside a `<button>`, so it must be presentational only.

slots.name: suffix
slots.description: Trailing content inside the trigger button, in `<span class="t-date-picker__slot t-date-picker__slot--suffix">`. Unlike `prefix` the wrapper is always rendered; supplying the slot replaces the default calendar icon. No scoped props, and the same presentational-only restriction applies.

a11y: accessible_name: None is generated for the trigger beyond its own text content, which is the formatted date or the placeholder — that changes as the user picks, so it is not a stable label. Pass `aria-label` (see example). The `id` prop cannot be used to link an external <label>.

useWhen: The value is a date without time.
avoidWhen: Time selection matters.

alternatives.when: The value must include time.

notes: Bind `modelValue` to a `YYYY-MM-DD` string.
Use `min` and `max` with the same format.

components.name: TDateTimePicker
components.purpose: Date and time picker with a calendar grid, time selection, and overlay trigger.

props.name: modelValue
props.note: Always emitted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm`. On input it also parses `YYYY-MM-DD` (midnight) and `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss`; anything else parses to null and renders the placeholder.

props.name: size
props.note: Renders as `t-date-picker--<size>` on the root — there is no `t-date-time-picker--<size>` modifier.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Blocks opening via click/keyboard and suppresses the overlay entirely, even when `open` is true.

props.name: loading
props.note: Only swaps the default suffix icon for a TSpinner; the trigger stays enabled and interactive.

props.name: locale
props.note: Feeds all four Intl.DateTimeFormat calls: trigger text, draft preview, month caption, weekday labels.

props.name: min
props.note: `YYYY-MM-DD` (resolved to 00:00 of that day) or `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm`.

props.name: max
props.note: `YYYY-MM-DD` (resolved to 23:59:59.999 of that day) or `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm`.

props.name: weekStartsOn
props.note: 0 = Sunday. Reorders the weekday header labels, the grid start date, and Home/End day navigation.

props.name: id
props.note: Id of the overlay dialog only — not of the root or the trigger. Read once at setup, so later changes are ignored. The trigger points at it with aria-controls while open.

props.name: step
props.note: Seconds between minute options. Must be a positive multiple of 60 below 3600; any other value falls back to 1-minute options. Hours are always 0-23 regardless of step.

a11y: aria_modal: false — the overlay is non-modal and does not trap focus
focus_restores_on_close: Only when closing via Apply, Clear, or Escape from a day button. Closing by clicking the trigger, Escape on the trigger, or an outside pointerdown leaves focus where it is.
behavior: draft_then_commit: Choosing a day and changing the Hour/Minute selects mutate an internal draft only. Nothing is emitted and the overlay does NOT close until Apply — unlike TDatePicker, where selecting a day commits and closes.
apply_emit_order: update:modelValue, then change, then select — all carrying the same `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm` string.
clear_emits_empty_string: Clear emits update:modelValue, change, and select with `''`, then closes.
default_draft_when_value_is_empty: Now, rounded UP to the next `step` boundary (minimum 60s) with seconds zeroed, then clamped into min/max.

useWhen: The value must include both date and time.
The user should pick a schedule from one compact control.
avoidWhen: Only a day is needed.

alternatives.when: Only the date matters.

notes: Bind `modelValue` to a `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm` string.
Use `step` in seconds. Values divisible by 60 produce minute increments.
Use `min` and `max` in the same date-time format.

components.name: TDescriptionList
components.purpose: Key/value description list with real <dl>/<dt>/<dd> semantics — the label↔value rows repeated across status/config/detail panels. Distinct from TList (<ul>/<li> content rows): a description list announces label/value pairs to assistive tech.

props.name: size
props.note: Row padding scale. Cascades to TDescriptionItem.

slots.name: default
slots.description: The TDescriptionItem rows.

behavior: responsive: On a container wider than 30rem the label and value sit side by side (value truncates). On a narrower container the label moves ABOVE the value — right for long values like ARNs (CSS container query; the list sets container-type).
semantics: Real <dl>/<dt>/<dd>; each item wraps its dt/dd in a <div>, which is valid grouping inside <dl>.

components.name: TDivider
components.description: Horizontal or vertical visual separator. Renders a single native <hr> and nothing else — there is no default slot, so the "text in the middle of a rule" pattern (an "OR" divider) is NOT supported; compose that yourself from a flex row plus TText. The `label` prop is an accessible name only, never visible text. There are no size, variant, tone, or spacing props.

props.name: orientation
props.description: Selects the `t-divider--{orientation}` modifier — the only class the component varies. `horizontal` spans the full inline width (width 100%, height 0) with `margin-block` spacing. `vertical` is a zero-width rule (width 0, height auto) that takes its height from `align-self: stretch`, so it is only visible inside a flex or grid row — in a plain block container it collapses to zero height and renders nothing.

props.name: decorative
props.description: When true (the default) renders `role="none"` and no ARIA attributes at all — purely visual, removed from the accessibility tree, and the `label` prop is dropped. When false renders `role="separator"` plus `aria-orientation`, and `aria-label` when `label` is set.

props.name: label
props.description: Accessible name, applied as `aria-label` ONLY when `decorative` is false. Because `decorative` defaults to true, `<TDivider label="Section" />` on its own renders no label at all — pass `:decorative="false"` as well. Never rendered as visible text.

a11y: role: "none" when decorative (the default), "separator" when `decorative` is false.

aria-orientation: Set to the `orientation` value only when `decorative` is false; omitted otherwise. Note that "horizontal" is already the implicit default for `role="separator"`, so it is redundant there but harmless.

aria-label: Set from the `label` prop only when `decorative` is false. Omitted when `label` is unset, so a non-decorative divider without a label is an unnamed separator (valid — separators need no name).

notes: The decorative default is the right one for most dividers: a purely visual rule should not be announced. Use `:decorative="false"` only when the rule genuinely separates two sections that assistive technology should perceive as distinct, and pair it with `label` when the sections need naming.
behavior: Renders a native <hr> element as its single root node.
Extra attrs and class/style fall through to that <hr> (attribute inheritance is not disabled).
Applies exactly two classes; no state, size, or variant modifiers exist.
A vertical divider needs a flex or grid parent to have any height.

components.name: TDonutChart
components.purpose: Part-of-whole chart rendered as a donut (default) or full pie, with a legend, percentages, an interactive center readout, and an accessible label.

props.name: segments
props.note: Each item is { label: string; value: number; color?: string }. Values <= 0 are filtered out before anything renders — they reach neither the chart, the legend, nor the segment count in the auto aria-label. Because the palette cycles by *visible* index, dropping a non-positive item shifts the colors of every segment after it.

props.name: size
props.note: Square canvas in px; applied to both the svg and the figure box. Donut geometry insets the ring by thickness/2 + 3px so the hover growth cannot clip; pie geometry uses a radius of size/2 - 2.

props.name: thickness
props.note: Ring thickness in px. `0` switches the whole render mode: segments become filled wedges to the center (a pie) and the center block is not rendered at all. Hovering a donut segment temporarily draws it at thickness + 4.

props.name: gap
props.note: Gap between slices, in degrees. In donut mode it is converted to arc length on the mid-radius circle and then floored — the effective gap is at least 2px, or at least thickness + 2px when rounded — so small values (including 0) still leave a visible seam. Forced to 0 whenever exactly one segment is visible.

props.name: rounded
props.note: Round the ends of donut segments (widens the gap to make room). Best with a thinner ring. Donut only — stroke-linecap is not applied to pie wedges, so it is ignored when thickness is 0.

props.name: showLegend
props.note: The legend only renders when there is data — it is suppressed in the empty state even when true, but it is NOT suppressed while loading, so real values sit next to the skeleton. The has-legend root class tracks the prop alone, not whether the legend actually rendered.

props.name: showPercent
props.note: Legend only. Percentages are rounded to whole numbers, so they may not sum to exactly 100%.

props.name: centerLabel
props.note: Static label under the center value. Falls back to the literal string 'Total' when unset, and is replaced by the hovered segment's label while a segment is hovered. Donut only; the center block is not rendered for a pie.

props.name: centerValue
props.note: Overrides the auto total in the center readout; falls back to the formatted sum of the visible values. Interpolated as-is (no valueFormat). Lower precedence than hover: hovering a segment replaces it with that segment's formatted value. Donut only.

props.name: valueFormat
props.note: Applies to the legend values and to the center value. Not applied to percentages, nor to an explicitly supplied centerValue.

props.name: loading
props.note: Replaces the figure with a pulsing skeleton disc and adds the is-loading root class. Takes precedence over the empty state; the legend still renders when data is present.

props.name: ariaLabel
props.default: auto-generated summary ('Donut chart with <n> segments' / 'Pie chart with <n> segments')
props.note: Applied to the inner <svg role="img">, which only exists in the data state — neither the loading nor the empty state carries it.

a11y: The inner <svg> carries role=img plus the ariaLabel (or the auto-generated summary); the root div has no role or accessible name.
Legend + percentages give a non-visual reading of each slice.
The hover readout is pointer-only — segments are not focusable and there is no keyboard equivalent, so never make it the only way to reach a value. The legend already carries the same numbers.
Legend swatches are aria-hidden, so color is never the only carrier of identity.
Provide a meaningful ariaLabel describing what the chart shows.
behavior: colors_auto_assign_from_chart_palette: --tree-color-chart-1..8, cycling by visible-segment index; per-segment color overrides
hovering_a_slice_dims_the_rest_and_updates_center: the other segments get is-dimmed, the hovered donut segment grows to thickness + 4, and its legend row gets is-active
hovering_a_legend_row_drives_the_same_highlight: legend items carry the same pointerenter/pointerleave handlers as the segments
center_readout_precedence: hovered segment > centerValue > formatted total; the label half is hovered segment label > centerLabel > 'Total'
single_segment_drops_the_gap: with exactly one visible segment the gap is forced to 0 in both donut and pie mode, so the ring/disc closes
empty_state_when_no_visible_segment_has_value: total <= 0 renders the empty slot instead of the svg; loading wins over it
segments_fade_in_staggered: 70ms animation delay per segment index; removed under prefers-reduced-motion

useWhen: You are showing how a total splits into a handful of categories.
A donut center readout (total or hovered slice) is useful.
avoidWhen: There are many categories or precise comparison matters — use TChart bars.
The data is a trend over time — use TChart or TSparkline.

alternatives.when: Precise magnitude comparison across categories is needed (bars).

notes: Pass `segments` as `{ label, value, color? }[]`; non-positive values are dropped.
Set `thickness: 0` for a full pie; keep the default for a donut with a center readout.
Use the `center` slot (donut only) for a custom center; otherwise it shows the total or the hovered slice.

components.name: TDrawer
components.purpose: Side-anchored panel for secondary content, navigation, or forms.

props.name: open
props.note: Runtime default is `undefined`. While it holds anything other than `undefined` the drawer is fully controlled — the internal state is ignored and the panel only moves when the parent updates this prop.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.note: Uncontrolled initial state. Read once on setup — later changes are ignored. Only meaningful while `open` is undefined. Opening this way skips the on-open side effects; see behavior.initial_open_skips_open_side_effects.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Only blocks opening from the `trigger` slot — the trigger wrapper gets `aria-disabled` and its click is swallowed (preventDefault + stopPropagation). It does NOT block a programmatic open through `open`/`defaultOpen`, and it disables nothing inside the drawer.

props.name: size
props.note: Applied as `t-drawer__surface--<size>`. Sizes the cross axis of the panel: width for `side: left | right`, height for `side: top | bottom`.

props.name: side
props.note: Applied as `t-drawer__surface--<side>`. Also selects the slide-in translate direction and which corners are rounded.

props.name: title
props.note: Rendered as `t-drawer__title` inside the fallback content of the `header` slot. An empty title with no `header` slot removes the header block entirely and leaves `aria-labelledby` unset — the dialog then has no accessible name.

props.name: description
props.note: Rendered as `t-drawer__description` between the topbar and the body, and referenced by `aria-describedby`. Only rendered when non-empty. The `content`/`default` body is NOT part of the accessible description.

props.name: closeOnEscape
props.note: Escape is handled on the surface's own keydown, so it only fires while focus is inside the drawer. When enabled the handler also calls preventDefault.

props.name: closeOnOverlay
props.note: Applies to clicks on `t-drawer__backdrop` only. The backdrop is a sibling of the surface, not an ancestor, so clicks inside the panel never reach it regardless of this prop.

props.name: showCloseButton
props.note: The topbar renders when `title` or the `header` slot is present OR this is true. Setting it false with no title and no `header` slot collapses the topbar entirely.

props.name: closeLabel
props.note: Accessible name (aria-label) of the header close button, which is icon-only and has no text. Only rendered when `showCloseButton` is true. The component's only localisation hook.

props.name: id
props.note: Id of the dialog surface; seeds `<id>-title` and `<id>-description`. Read once on setup (not reactive). Auto-generated via `createId('t-drawer')` when omitted.

slots.name: trigger
slots.description: Element that opens the drawer on click. Omit for controlled or imperative use.

slots.name: header
slots.description: Replaces the default title heading in the topbar.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Drawer body content.

slots.name: content
slots.description: Drawer body content; takes precedence over the default slot.

slots.name: footer
slots.description: Action row pinned under the body.

a11y: restores_focus_on_close: {"value":"partial","detail":"The previously focused element is recorded only in the trigger-click path. A drawer opened programmatically — by setting `open` from elsewhere, or by `defaultOpen` — records nothing, so focus is not restored on close. Move focus back yourself in that case.\n"}
escape_scope: The keydown listener lives on the surface, so Escape only closes while focus is inside the drawer.
accessible_name_source: the `t-drawer__header` div, via aria-labelledby; unset when `title` is empty and no `header` slot is supplied
accessible_description_source: the `description` prop, via aria-describedby; the body slots are not included
initial_focus: First focusable element inside the surface, else the surface itself (`tabindex=\"-1\"`, with a visible `:focus-visible` ring). Applied on the closed → open transition only.

trigger_state_not_announced: The trigger wrapper gets no aria-haspopup, aria-expanded, or aria-controls, and no scoped props are exposed to build them. A trigger that needs to announce the drawer must wire those attributes from consumer state.
behavior: root_uses_display_contents_without_trigger: `t-drawer` is `display: inline-flex`, but a root with no `t-drawer__trigger` child switches to `display: contents` so the empty wrapper does not generate a line box and add stray height to the page.
topbar_rendered_when: title is non-empty OR the `header` slot is supplied OR showCloseButton is true
data_state_attribute: `data-state="open" | "closed"` on both the `t-drawer` root and the `t-drawer__portal`
focus_trap_detail: Tab and Shift+Tab cycle within the surface. With no focusable descendants, Tab is swallowed and focus is pushed back to the surface itself.

initial_open_skips_open_side_effects: {"value":true,"detail":"The on-open work — body scroll lock and initial focus — runs from a watcher on the open state that is not immediate, so a drawer that starts open via `defaultOpen: true` (or an `open` prop already true on first render) never locks scroll and never moves focus into the surface. Focus trap and Escape therefore stay dormant until the user tabs in. Start closed and open on the next tick if you need the full behavior.\n"}

body_scroll_lock_scope: {"value":"per_component_instance","detail":"The overlay counter and the saved `overflow` value are per instance, not shared across overlays. Two overlays open at once each restore what they individually saw, so closing the first-opened one while a second is still open unlocks page scroll early.\n"}

useWhen: The user needs a larger contextual panel while keeping page context visible.
avoidWhen: The task requires full modal focus or only a small anchored panel.

alternatives.when: The task should be fully blocking.
A compact anchored panel is enough.

components.name: TDropdown
components.purpose: Trigger + floating menu with keyboard navigation for action lists.

props.name: items
props.note: Array of { label: string; value: string; disabled?: boolean }. Each entry renders one `<li role="menuitem">`, keyed by `value` — values must be unique, because the internal element map that drives focus is also keyed by `value` and duplicates collide. With the default `[]` the component still renders an empty `<ul role="menu">` and focus stays on the trigger; there is no empty-state affordance (no `emptyText`, no `empty` slot), so guard on `items.length` yourself.

props.name: open
props.note: Controlled open state. Once a boolean is passed the component stops tracking open state internally — it only emits update:open / open-change and the consumer MUST write the value back, or the menu never opens. Flipping it externally does not run the open routine: no item is focused and the outside-click listener is only attached from the transition, so an initially-`true` value opens the menu with focus untouched.

props.name: defaultOpen
props.note: Uncontrolled initial open state, read once on mount. Later changes to it are ignored. It renders the menu already open but does NOT focus the first item (focusedIndex stays -1, so no `is-focused` row) and does NOT arm the outside-click listener until the open state changes at least once — see behavior.outside_click_closes_menu.

props.name: disabled
props.note: Sets the native `disabled` attribute on the built-in trigger button, adds `is-disabled` to the root, and suppresses the menu entirely (`v-if="isOpen && !disabled"`) — even while `open` is true. Also short-circuits openMenu, toggleMenu and the trigger keydown handler. It is bound only to the default button, so a custom `trigger` slot must render its own disabled state.

props.name: size
props.note: Sizes the trigger and the menu rows. `t-dropdown--md` is emitted but has no CSS rule of its own — it is the base `.t-dropdown__trigger` / `.t-dropdown__item` styling.

props.name: label
props.note: Text of the built-in trigger button AND the menu's accessible name (`:aria-label="label || undefined"` on the `<ul role="menu">`). A custom `trigger` slot does not render `label`, but it still names the menu — keep passing it, or the menu has no accessible name at all.

props.name: align
props.note: Horizontal alignment of the menu relative to the trigger — `start` pins `left: 0`, `end` pins `right: 0`. Use `end` when the trigger sits near the right edge of a clipping container (e.g. a table actions column). There is no collision detection: the menu is a plain absolutely-positioned child of the root, so it never flips and is clipped by any ancestor with `overflow: hidden`.

props.name: id
props.note: Custom id for the menu element. Auto-generated when omitted; also exposed to the `trigger` slot as `menuId` so a custom trigger can wire aria-controls. Resolved once during setup — it is NOT reactive, so changing `id` after mount has no effect.

events.name: select
events.description: Emitted with the chosen item's `value`. Disabled items never emit. Fires before the menu closes; the close that follows restores focus to the trigger.

events.name: update:open
events.description: Emitted whenever the open state changes. Deduped — no emit when the next value equals the current one.

events.name: open-change
events.description: Emitted immediately after `update:open`, with the same payload. Alias for consumers that do not use v-model:open.

a11y: custom_trigger_caveat: The three trigger_aria_* guarantees above describe the BUILT-IN trigger button only. A custom `trigger` slot replaces that button, so the consumer must bind them from the `isOpen` and `menuId` scoped props — see slots.trigger.
behavior: outside_click_closes_menu: {"value":true,"detail":"Detected on a document `pointerdown` listener that is attached by a watcher on the open state. The watcher is not immediate, so a menu that is already open on mount — via `defaultOpen` or an initially-`true` `open` — has no listener until the open state changes at least once, and outside clicks do not close it.\n"}

opening_focuses_first_enabled_item: {"value":true,"detail":"Applies to the open routine only — a click or Enter/Space/ArrowDown/ArrowUp on the trigger. `defaultOpen` and an externally driven `open` skip it, leaving focusedIndex at -1 with no row focused and no `is-focused` class. With an empty `items` array focus stays on the trigger; when every item is disabled, focus lands on the first (disabled) item.\n"}

keyboard_activation_on_the_trigger_never_closes_the_menu: {"value":true,"detail":"Clicking the trigger toggles, but Enter/Space/Arrow keys always call the open routine, so pressing them while the menu is open re-focuses the first enabled item instead of closing. Escape is the keyboard close.\n"}

focus_restores_on_close: {"value":true,"detail":"Focus returns to the trigger when the menu closes from inside it — selecting an item or pressing Escape on an item. Closing from the trigger itself (clicking it again, or Escape while the trigger holds focus) does not run the restore, because focus is already there. It deliberately does NOT move focus on an outside click, where the pointer has already placed focus elsewhere.\n","applies_to_custom_trigger":true}

useWhen: A button should open a short list of actions or choices.
avoidWhen: The user is choosing a form value with v-model.

alternatives.when: The dropdown is a form control for selecting a value.
The menu opens from a context action.

components.name: TEmptyState
components.purpose: Structured empty, no-results, and first-run placeholder block with optional icon, body content, and actions.

useWhen: A screen or section has no content and needs guidance.
avoidWhen: You only need a brief inline warning.

alternatives.when: The layout already has content and only needs a message.

components.name: TFileUpload
components.purpose: Composable dropzone and file picker with browse, drag-and-drop, and Ctrl+V paste support, plus per-file upload state: progress, remaining time, thumbnails, and independent per-file retry/resume.

props.name: disabled
props.description: The ONLY prop that gates interaction. Disables the dropzone, the native input, Clear all, and every per-row Remove/Retry, and makes the paste and drop handlers early-return.

props.name: loading
props.description: Presentational only. Applies `is-loading`, `aria-busy` on the dropzone, and the corner spinner. It gates NO interaction — see behavior.loading_is_presentational.

props.name: maxFiles
props.description: Cap on the TOTAL list size, not on one batch — remaining slots are counted against the files already selected. `multiple: false` overrides it with a hard cap of 1. Values below 0 are clamped to 0, which rejects everything.

props.name: maxFileSize
props.description: Per-file byte cap. Enforced only when it is a number greater than 0 — both `null` and `0` disable the check.

props.name: description
props.default: Click to browse, drag and drop, or paste with Ctrl+V.

props.name: uploadState
props.description: Per-file transport state, keyed by File OBJECT IDENTITY. Accepts a ReadonlyMap<File, TFileUploadState> or a (file, index) => TFileUploadState | undefined resolver. Omit it and the component renders exactly as before: no bars, no status lines, no retry.

props.name: dragLabel
props.description: Replaces `label` in the dropzone while an acceptable drag is over it. Set it to the same value as `label` to opt out of the text swap.

props.name: dragRejectLabel
props.default: This file type is not accepted
props.description: Replaces `label` while a drag that cannot work is over the dropzone. Only reachable when drag-reject detection is possible — see behavior.drag_reject_detection_is_mime_only.

props.name: thumbnails
props.description: Mint object-URL previews and decode dimensions for image/* files. Set false when you always supply `state.thumbnailUrl` yourself.

props.name: maxThumbnailSize
props.description: Skip auto-thumbnailing above this byte size. null removes the cap.

props.name: resumeLabel
props.description: Receives Math.round(state.progress). Used only when state.resumable === true AND state.progress is a number.

props.name: remainingTimeFormat
props.description: Formats state.remainingMs. Ignored for a row that supplies state.remainingLabel.

props.name: statusLabels
props.description: MERGED over the defaults { pending: Waiting, uploading: Uploading, paused: Paused, error: Upload failed, success: Uploaded } — pass only the keys you override.

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted with the next file list when files are added, removed, or cleared.

events.name: files-accepted
events.description: Emitted with only the newly accepted files, after accept/maxFiles/maxFileSize validation.

events.name: files-rejected
events.description: Pre-acceptance validation failures. Unrelated to transport errors, which you report through `uploadState`.

events.name: retry
events.description: Emitted when a row's retry affordance is activated. Pure intent — the component performs no upload. Use `payload.mode` ('resume' | 'restart'); it reports the affordance the component ACTUALLY DREW, so never re-derive it from your own state.

events.name: cancel
events.description: Emitted ONLY for rows whose status is 'uploading' or 'paused', immediately BEFORE the `update:modelValue` that drops them — so the file is still in the list when your handler runs and you can abort the request. `reason` is 'remove' for one row or 'clear' (one event per in-flight file) for Clear all.

slots.name: icon
slots.description: Replaces the dropzone icon.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Replaces the dropzone body. Receives `{ files, isDragActive, isDragReject, openFileDialog, clearFiles }`.

slots.name: file
slots.description: Full row override. Receives all of TFileUploadFileSlotProps, including every derived label, so a custom row re-derives nothing.

slots.name: file-status
slots.description: Replaces ONLY the progress bar + status line + error block inside the default row. Receives TFileUploadFileSlotProps. Name, meta, thumbnail, Retry, and Remove keep rendering. Ignored when the `file` slot is provided.

slots.name: actions
slots.description: Footer actions. Receives `{ files, openFileDialog, clearFiles }` — no upload state.

a11y: per_row_progressbar_is_labelled_with_status_and_filename: e.g. 'Uploading hero.png' — every row's bar has a distinct accessible name.
terminal_transitions_announced_with_filenames: One polite root live region (`role=log`) announces only success/error, e.g. 'alpha.txt uploaded.' / 'alpha.txt failed. Connection lost.'

repeated_identical_messages_are_still_announced: The log appends each message as a new node rather than overwriting one string, so a retry that fails with the byte-identical error still mutates the region and is announced. It is not aria-atomic: only the appended message is read, and the trimmed history (5 most recent) is not.
percentages_are_never_announced: Percent and ETA live in non-live text; the value reaches assistive tech through the progressbar's aria-valuenow.
focus_is_rescued_when_retry_unmounts: Flipping error -> uploading removes the focused Retry; focus moves to that row's Remove (or the dropzone), and only when it was genuinely lost.
behavior: upload_state_is_keyed_by_file_identity: Both source shapes key on the File object, never on index or name. Key your own store with the File objects you receive from `files-accepted`.

a_lookup_miss_renders_nothing: An unknown File yields `undefined` and the row falls back to name + meta only — no bar, no status, no retry. This fail-blank is deliberate: it is the back-compat path and it fails loudly instead of showing another file's percentage.

loading_is_presentational: BREAKING since 0.14.0 — `loading` no longer disables anything. It was freezing the dropzone, Clear all, and every Remove, which blocked removing file 1 while file 4 uploaded. Use `:disabled` to reproduce the old behavior; prefer `uploadState` and freeze nothing.
each_file_has_an_independent_track: One row's error, progress, or retry never gates another's. Remove stays enabled for in-flight rows and emits `cancel` so you can abort.
errors_are_per_row: Transport errors go in `state.error` and render under their own row. They never enter the dropzone feedback list, which Remove and Clear all wipe.
drag_feedback_has_three_signals: Border, glow, and a text swap to `dragLabel` — all three fire together, including when files are already listed.

drag_reject_detection_is_mime_only: `is-drag-reject` + `dragRejectLabel` require every `accept` token to be a MIME token; with any `.ext` token the dropzone stays neutral, because a drag exposes only `.type`. A mixed drag (one good file, one bad) is NOT rejected, and the drop is never blocked — rejected files always get the normal `files-rejected` explanation.

progress_bar_visibility: Shown on uploading/paused/error, and on success only with numeric progress. 'uploading' without progress is indeterminate; paused/error without progress is a determinate 0 — an errored bar must never animate.
the_bar_is_never_reset_on_error: It holds `state.progress` in the error tone. That retained 90% is what makes the resume promise legible.
remaining_time_strings: < 5000ms -> 'Less than 5s left'; < 60s -> 'About 12s left'; < 60min -> 'About 2 min left'; else 'About 2h left'. Negative, NaN, and Infinity render nothing.
status_line_composition: Joins the present parts with ' · ': 'Uploading · 62% · About 12s left' while uploading, 'Upload failed · 2.1 MB of 2.4 MB uploaded' on a resumable error.
thumbnails_are_client_only: Object URLs are minted after mount and revoked on removal and unmount. SSR and the hydrating render both show the extension chip, so there is no mismatch.

notes: Bind `modelValue` to `File[]`.
Use `accept`, `multiple`, and size limits in the app layer to constrain uploads.

components.name: TFlag
components.purpose: Render a flat country flag image from a CDN at a fixed height, centred in a 3:2 box, with a text fallback when the request fails.

props.name: code
props.description: ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code. Case-insensitive — trimmed and lowercased before the URL is built.

props.name: baseUrl
props.description: Origin serving the flag assets. The component appends a fixed path built from the CDN's fixed-height segments, so a self-hosted mirror must reproduce it exactly: 1x is `${baseUrl}/h20/{code}.png` (sm), `${baseUrl}/h20/{code}.png` (md), `${baseUrl}/h24/{code}.png` (lg); the matching 2x asset is emitted via srcset as `${baseUrl}/h40/{code}.png` (sm), `${baseUrl}/h40/{code}.png` (md), `${baseUrl}/h60/{code}.png` (lg). A trailing slash is stripped, so `https://cdn.example.com/flags/` and `https://cdn.example.com/flags` behave identically.

props.name: size
props.description: Box height, taken from `--tree-size-icon-sm/md/lg` (16px / 20px / 24px); the box width is 3:2 of that height. Also selects which CDN asset pair is requested — see `baseUrl`.

props.name: label
props.description: Accessible label. When set, the flag becomes role="img" with aria-label; otherwise it is aria-hidden. An empty string counts as unset — the flag stays decorative.

props.name: fallbackText
props.description: Text shown when the image cannot load. Defaults to the normalized `code` uppercased, so a padded `" br "` falls back to `BR`.

a11y: image_alt_is_always_empty: The `` always carries `alt=""`, so the accessible name comes only from `label` on the root span — never from the image itself.

fallback_text_is_hidden_when_decorative: The fallback text renders inside the root span, so without `label` it inherits `aria-hidden="true"` and is not announced. It is a visual affordance, not an accessible name.

consumer_aria_label_does_not_replace_label: Fallthrough attributes land on the same root span, so a consumer-supplied `aria-label` overrides the one built from `label` — but it does not set `role="img"` or clear `aria-hidden="true"`, leaving the flag hidden and the label unannounced. Use the `label` prop instead.
rule: Provide `label` only when the flag carries meaning that is not already in adjacent text — inside a language option the label is redundant.
behavior: fixed_height_endpoints_serve_flat_artwork_at_true_proportions: The CDN's ratio-normalised (width-by-height) endpoints do force every flag into one box, but only by serving the WAVING artwork, which reads as decoration next to flat UI icons. The fixed-height endpoints used here (h20, h24, h40, h60) serve the flat artwork at each flag's true proportions instead: Nepal stays a pennant, Switzerland stays square, Qatar stays elongated.

shared_height_not_shared_ratio_is_what_aligns_a_column: Within a size step every flag is requested at the same asset height, so a stacked list lines up even though the widths differ. CSS then places each flag in a fixed 3:2 box (3:2 being the most common national flag ratio) with `object-fit: contain`, so the majority fill the box and the outliers centre inside it rather than being cropped or distorted.
retina_asset_is_at_least_twice_the_box_height: Box heights come from `--tree-size-icon-sm/md/lg` (16px / 20px / 24px) and the srcset asset is h40 (sm, md) or h60 (lg), so a 2x screen never upscales.
size_outside_the_union_falls_back_to_md: A JS consumer can pass a size outside `TSize`; the asset lookup falls back to the md pair rather than throwing on an undefined entry.
base_url_trailing_slash_is_normalised: Trailing slashes are stripped from `baseUrl` before the path is appended, so a self-hosted mirror cannot silently produce a double-slashed URL.

sm_and_md_share_one_asset_pair: sm and md both request h20 (h40 at 2x); only lg differs (h24, h60 at 2x). Four distinct segments exist in total, not one pair per size step, so a mirror needs four directories per flag.
path_template_is_public_contract: Changing `baseUrl` is a config change, not a breaking one, as long as the mirror serves the same fixed-height segments (h20, h24, h40, h60).
image_element_is_removed_not_hidden_on_failure: The `` is behind `v-if`, so a failed load takes it out of the DOM and swaps in the fallback span — it is not left in place with `display: none`.
no_country_or_locale_dataset_is_shipped: TreeUI never maps a language or locale to a country code — the application owns that decision.

useWhen: You need a country flag rendered from an ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code.
Flags stacked in a list must share one height so rows stay aligned — the flat assets keep each flag's true proportions and centre inside a fixed 3:2 box.
You want a text fallback when the flag asset cannot load.
avoidWhen: The flag would be the only carrier of meaning — always pair it with adjacent text such as the language or country name.
You need an interface glyph rather than a country flag.
You are treating the flag as a language identity — a flag names a country, not a language.

alternatives.when: The graphic is a registered TreeUI icon, not a country flag.
The flag belongs to a language or locale picker rather than standing alone.

required: Pass `code` as an ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code. Matching is case-insensitive — the value is trimmed and lowercased internally.
Allow `https://flagcdn.com` in the app `Content-Security-Policy` `img-src` directive, or point `baseUrl` at a self-hosted mirror — flag images are fetched from a third-party CDN at runtime.
notes: A mirror must serve `{baseUrl}/{segment}/{code}.png` for both steps of every size: 1x `h20` (sm), `h20` (md), `h24` (lg); 2x `h40` (sm), `h40` (md), `h60` (lg). The 2x asset is emitted through `srcset`.
Those fixed-height segments are part of the contract, and they are the CDN's flat artwork — a mirror must serve flat flags, not the waving ones.
Do not substitute the CDN's ratio-normalised width-by-height endpoints. They equalise aspect ratio only by switching to the waving artwork, which reads as decoration next to flat UI icons.
The fixed-height assets keep each flag's true proportions — Nepal is a pennant, Switzerland is square, Qatar is elongated. A shared height, not a shared ratio, is what aligns a column of flags.
CSS puts the image in a fixed 3:2 box (3:2 being the most common national flag ratio) with `object-fit: contain`, so outliers centre inside the box instead of being cropped or distorted.
Each retina segment is at least twice its box height — boxes are `--tree-size-icon-sm/md/lg` = 16px/20px/24px — so 2x screens never upscale.
`baseUrl` may be written with or without a trailing slash; trailing slashes are stripped before the path is built. An unknown `size` falls back to the `md` assets.
When the image cannot load — offline, blocked by CSP, unknown code — the component renders the uppercased `code` as text. Override that text with `fallbackText`.
The flag is `aria-hidden` by default. Pass `label` only when the flag carries meaning on its own; beside a visible country or language name it should stay decorative.

components.name: TFormField
components.description: Label + input wrapper with error and hint slots. Provides consistent layout, labelling, and validation messaging for any form control.

props.name: label
props.description: Text content for the field label.

props.name: htmlFor
props.description: Rendered verbatim as the <label for> attribute. There is no id generation: when omitted, the label carries no `for` and is NOT associated with any control. The consumer must pass this AND set the same value as `id` on the child control.

props.name: error
props.description: Error message to display. When present, the hint is hidden and the field gets is-invalid state.

props.name: hint
props.description: Hint or help text displayed below the control. Hidden when error is present.

props.name: required
props.description: Adds a visual required indicator (*) next to the label.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Applies disabled styling (reduced opacity) to the entire field.

props.name: size
props.description: Controls the label font size.

slots.name: default
slots.description: The form control (e.g. TInput, TCheckbox, TSelect).

slots.name: label
slots.description: Custom label content. Overrides the label prop text.

slots.name: error
slots.description: Custom error content. Overrides the error prop text.

slots.name: hint
slots.description: Custom hint content. Overrides the hint prop text.

a11y: Renders a native <label> element, but ONLY when `label` or the `label` slot is provided.
Error messages are wrapped in role='alert' for screen reader announcements.
Required indicator (*) is aria-hidden to avoid redundant announcements; use aria-required on the input itself.
Error takes precedence over hint to avoid conflicting messages.
Extra attrs land on the root <div> (inheritAttrs: false), not on the child control.

useWhen: You need consistent label, hint, and error layout around a control.
avoidWhen: You need the actual input control.

alternatives.when: You need a text entry control.
You need a dropdown control.

required: Wrap a control such as `TInput`, `TSelect`, `TCombobox`, `TCheckbox`, or `TDatePicker`.
Pass `htmlFor` matching the child control `id` when the child exposes one.
notes: Set the child control invalid state separately when `error` is present.

components.name: TGrid
components.purpose: Responsive grid wrapper for cards, forms, stats, and other multi-column layouts.
a11y: Acts as a layout wrapper only; semantics come from child content and optional `as` prop.

useWhen: Children should flow into responsive columns.
You need stat grids, card grids, or form rows.
avoidWhen: You only need one-dimensional spacing.

alternatives.when: A single row or column is enough.

components.name: TIcon
components.purpose: Render a registered TreeUI icon by name with consistent sizing and accessibility defaults.

props.name: name
props.description: Icon identifier from the @treeui/icons registry. Widens as `TIconRegistry` is augmented, so registered app icons are valid values too.
props.note: Names use TreeUI's descriptive kebab-case vocabulary. The catalog covers common application needs; anything missing is one `registerTreeIcons` call away, and `listTreeIcons()` returns the live list at runtime.

props.name: size
props.description: Pixel size for both width and height of the rendered svg.
props.note:
This is a PIXEL COUNT, not the sm | md | lg token scale used by nearly every
other TreeUI component. `<TIcon size="md" />` does not fail loudly: it writes
width="md" / height="md" onto the svg, which the browser discards, so the
icon becomes unsized and stretches to fill its container. Pass a number
(20, 32) or a numeric string ("20"). To size an icon from tokens, leave `size`
alone and set width/height in CSS from the icon scale — --tree-size-icon-sm |
-md | -lg | -xl | -2xl (1rem / 1.25rem / 1.5rem / 2rem / 3rem) — which is what
TLanguageSelect's switcher variant does.

props.name: absoluteStrokeWidth
props.description: When true, stroke width stays visually constant across sizes.

props.name: label
props.description: Accessible label. When set, the icon becomes role="img" with aria-label; otherwise it is aria-hidden.

a11y: rule: Provide `label` only when the icon conveys meaning that is not already in adjacent text.
behavior: unknown_name_renders_nothing: An unregistered name makes `resolveTreeIcon` return `undefined`, and `TIcon`'s `v-if` then renders no element at all — no empty box, no placeholder, no error. A `console.warn` names the missing icon, suggests the `registerTreeIcons` call, and lists every valid name. It warns once per NAME (not per message — the message embeds the valid-name list, which changes on every registration), so a miss inside a render loop cannot flood the console. The warning is NOT gated on `process.env.NODE_ENV`: it fires in production builds too, deliberately, because a missing icon is a misconfiguration worth hearing about once and `process` is undefined in most browser bundles. Components with a marker fallback (TNavMenu) show that instead — see nav-menu.yaml.

absolute_stroke_width_math: Icons draw on `viewBox="0 0 24 24"` with the svg sized to `size`, so painted pixels = (stroke-width attribute) * size / 24. With `absoluteStrokeWidth` (the default) the attribute is set to `(strokeWidth * 24) / size`, which cancels the scale: at the default `strokeWidth: 2` the attribute is 2.4 at size 20, 1.5 at 32, and 1.0 at 48 — a constant 2px painted at every size. That is the entire point of the correction: a 48px icon sits beside a 20px one at the same optical weight. A `size` of 0 or a non-numeric value keeps the nominal stroke rather than producing NaN/Infinity. Setting `absoluteStrokeWidth: false` pins the attribute at `strokeWidth`, which is the hand-rolled behaviour: the painted stroke then scales WITH the icon (1.67px at 20, 2.67px at 32, 4px at 48 — double the library weight).

icons_are_built_lazily_and_cached: Geometry lives in a data registry; a component is created on first lookup and reused after that, so an unused icon stays as DATA instead of becoming a `defineComponent`. Note what this does not claim: the geometry for all 364 built-ins is retained by any import of the package (the registry copies `builtinTreeIconNodes` into a `Map` at module scope, so a live reference exists and nothing tree-shakes it away — `sideEffects: false` does not change that). The saving is component construction, not bundle bytes.
resolved_per_render: TIcon resolves its name in a computed, not once at setup, so an icon registered after the component mounted still appears.

migration_aliases: Twelve compatibility names resolve to the same geometry as their canonical names: grid → layout-grid, share → share-nodes, chevron-updown → chevrons-up-down, refresh → refresh-cw, comment/chat → message-circle, check-square → square-check, help → circle-help, automations → zap, connections → external-link, persona → user-round, and close → x.

company_logos_are_app_owned: Company logos are not built-ins. When an application needs one, it imports the official SVG as its own component and passes or registers that component under an application-owned name.

useWhen: You need to render an interface icon. `TIcon` is the way to render one — it resolves the name through the registry and applies the shared size, stroke, and a11y defaults.
The icon is one of the built-ins: `<TIcon name="cpu" />`. `listTreeIcons()` returns every registered name at runtime.
The icon is your own: register it once with `registerTreeIcons`, then use it by name exactly like a built-in. A missing icon is registered, not worked around.
avoidWhen: The graphic is a country flag — a flag names a country, not an interface concept.

A slot already renders the icon for you (`#icon` on TAlert, TBadge, TButton, TConfirmDialog, TEmptyState, TFileUpload, TStat, TTag) — put the `TIcon` inside that slot rather than beside it. None of those components has an `icon` PROP; the slot is the whole interface.

alternatives.component: a component passed straight to `TNavMenuItem.icon`, or rendered into an `#icon` slot
alternatives.when: You want the escape hatch with no registry entry — a one-off icon, an imported SVG component, or the result of `createTreeIcon(...)`. `TNavMenuItem.icon` is `TIconInput` and takes the component directly; everywhere else, render it inside the component's `#icon` slot. `TIcon` itself will not take a component — its `name` is `TIconName`. Prefer registering when the icon is used in more than one place.
The graphic is a country flag rendered from an ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code.

required: Use a registered name: one of the built-ins, or one the app added with `registerTreeIcons`. See `icons` above.
notes: `size` is a pixel count, not `sm | md | lg`. Passing `size="md"` renders an unsized svg.
The icon is `aria-hidden` by default. Pass `label` only when the icon carries meaning on its own; beside visible text it should stay decorative.

components.name: TInput
components.purpose: Text input with size, loading, invalid, and prefix or suffix slots.

props.name: width
props.note: Inline-size cap (max-inline-size). Fluid by default; use instead of an inline width style.

useWhen: The value is short, plain, and single-line.
avoidWhen: The content is multiline, numeric, or markdown.

alternatives.when: The content is multiline.
The value is numeric.
Rich markdown authoring is needed.

components.name: TKbd
components.purpose: Keyboard-key hint (e.g. ⌘K, Esc, Ctrl) rendered as a native <kbd> with the library's chip styling.
a11y: Uses the native <kbd> element so assistive tech announces it as keyboard input.

components.name: TLanguageSelect
components.purpose: Language/locale picker with a listbox, a flag per option, and the shared overlay contract; controlled and uncontrolled via v-model and open/defaultOpen.

props.name: options
props.note: Array of { label: string; value: string; code?: string; description?: string; disabled?: boolean }. `value` is an opaque application key (typically a BCP-47 tag) — never parsed or validated. `code` is an ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code for the flag; omit it to render the option without one. `description` is a secondary line, e.g. the endonym.

props.name: variant
props.note: `field` is a form control, sized and laid out like any other input. `switcher` is a page-level control for a navbar/appbar where nothing nearby labels it: it leads with a translate icon, trails the current flag, and sizes to its content.

props.name: iconOnly
props.note: `switcher` only — ignored by `field`. Drops the visible language name, leaving the translate icon, the current flag, and the chevron. The trigger then has no text, so the consumer MUST pass an `aria-label`.

props.name: width
props.note: Inline-size cap (max-inline-size). Fluid by default; use instead of an inline width style. Applies to `variant: field` only — a `switcher` sizes to its content and ignores it.

props.name: flagBaseUrl
props.note: Forwarded to every nested TFlag. When omitted, TFlag's own default origin applies. Set once here to self-host flag assets.

slots.name: prefix
slots.description: Leading content inside the trigger, before everything else — the selected flag and label in `field`, the translate icon in `switcher`.

slots.name: option
slots.description: Replaces the default option body (flag, label, description). Renders INSIDE the row's <li role="option">, so it must contain only presentational content.

slots.name: empty
slots.description: Replaces `emptyText` when `options` is empty.

useWhen: The user is picking the interface language or locale.
The control is a labelled form field for the language value — `variant="field"` (the default), where the flag leads the trigger and `width` applies.

The control is a page-level language switcher in a navbar or app bar, where nothing nearby says what it changes — `variant="switcher"` leads with a translate icon and trails the current flag, sizes to its content, and ignores `width`.
A navbar switcher should shrink to icon and flag only — `iconOnly` drops the language name, so pass an `aria-label` to name the control.
Each language should render with its country flag, and optionally an endonym as a second line.
The app owns the language list and applies the locale side effects itself.
avoidWhen: The values are not languages or locales.
The language list is long enough that the user must type to narrow it down.
You expect TreeUI to supply the locale dataset — it ships none, so pass your own `options`.

alternatives.when: The values are ordinary application data rather than languages or locales.
The language list is long enough that typing to narrow it down is required.
You only need to render one country flag, without a picker around it.

required: Pass `options` as `{ label, value, code?, description?, disabled? }[]`. TreeUI ships no language, locale, or country dataset — the app owns its language list.
Apply locale side effects (i18n locale, `lang` attribute, persistence) in the app layer; the component only reports the selected `value`.
Pass an `aria-label` whenever `iconOnly` is set — the visible language name is gone, so it is the control's only accessible name. It also labels the listbox.
notes: `variant` is `field` (default) or `switcher`, typed as `TLanguageSelectVariant` and exported from `@treeui/vue`.

Use `variant="field"` inside a form, next to other inputs, or wrapped in `TFormField`: the flag leads the trigger, the trigger fills its container, and `width` applies. A visible field label already says what the control changes.

Use `variant="switcher"` for a page-level control in a navbar or app bar, where nothing nearby labels it. A translate icon (`TIcon name="languages"`) leads and the current flag trails before the chevron, so the control reads as "page language, now set to this" without an external label.
A switcher sizes to its content, so `width` does not apply, and its listbox aligns to the trigger's right edge.
`iconOnly` is switcher-only and drops the visible language name, leaving the translate icon and the current flag.
`value` is an opaque application key, typically a BCP-47 tag such as `pt-BR`. TreeUI never parses or validates it.
`code` is the ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code used for the option flag. Omit it to render the option without a flag — a language is not a country, so many entries have no honest flag.
Use `description` for a secondary line such as the endonym.
Set `flagBaseUrl` once on the select rather than per option; it is forwarded to every nested `TFlag`.
Bind `modelValue` to a `string`, and use `v-model:open` when the app controls open state.
The CDN and Content-Security-Policy requirements under `TFlag` apply whenever any option carries a `code`.

components.name: TLink
components.purpose: Inline link with consistent styling, optional RouterLink rendering, and external link support.

props.name: to
props.description: Route target. When provided and vue-router is available, renders RouterLink.

props.name: href
props.description: Native href for anchor elements. Ignored when `to` is provided with vue-router.

props.name: external
props.description: Opens link in new tab with rel='noopener noreferrer'.

props.name: variant
props.note: Emphasis axis (colour), not decoration. There is deliberately no `plain` value — a decoration-free link uses `underline="none"` + `weight`, keeping `variant` a pure emphasis axis (a `plain` value would be a homonym, the mistake that removed `solid` from TCard).

props.name: underline
props.note: When the underline shows. `always` is the base; `hover` underlines only on hover; `none` removes it (e.g. for a nav item or an icon+label lockup).

props.name: weight
props.note: Text weight. `medium` matches the base link.

a11y: semantic_element: a (or RouterLink, or span when disabled)

useWhen: The action navigates somewhere — a URL, a route, or an anchor on the page.
You want consistent link styling with `default`, `muted`, or `danger` emphasis.
You want a component that renders RouterLink when vue-router is installed and a plain <a> when it is not, without branching in the template.
avoidWhen: The control performs an action rather than navigating (submit, open a dialog, delete) — that is a button, even if it looks like a link.
You need a full call-to-action surface with variants, sizes, and a loading state.

alternatives.when: The control performs an action. Use `variant="ghost"` for a low-emphasis, link-like button.
The links form a hierarchy trail.
The links are app-section navigation in a shell or sidebar.

components.name: TLinkTile
components.purpose: Interactive navigational tile — a single link surface with a leading region, title and description, a token-driven tone accent, and library-owned hover/focus/sizing. For app launchers, product grids and settings entries. Owns no grid: compose collections with TGrid.

props.name: title
props.note: Tile heading. Rendered via TText.

props.name: description
props.note: Supporting line under the title. Muted, smaller.

props.name: tone
props.note: Accent for the leading region and the current/hover border. A closed axis mapped to status/brand tokens — never a free colour or custom property. The soft tint is derived from the base token with color-mix, staying theme-aware.

props.name: size
props.note: Padding scale.

props.name: current
props.note: Marks this tile as the current destination — emits `aria-current="page"` and applies the accent surface.

props.name: href
props.note: Native href.

props.name: to
props.note: Route target. Renders as RouterLink when vue-router is available, else `<a>`. The tile is always a link (there is no non-link mode) — the whole surface is a single link with no nested secondary action.

slots.name: leading
slots.description: Leading visual, typically a TIcon. Wrapped in an `aria-hidden` accent container.

slots.name: default
slots.description: Extra content appended under the description.

a11y: single_link_no_nested_interactive: The entire tile is one link; do not nest a button/link inside — nested interactives inside a link are an accessibility trap.
accessible_name: Comes from the title (and description) text; supply `aria-label` if the tile is icon-only.
focus_visible: Focus ring on the tile.

components.name: TList
components.purpose: Non-selectable content list — plain <ul>/<li> rows with leading/content/meta/actions regions. Unlike TSelectableList (role=listbox / role=option), the rows carry no selection semantics, so the actions region can hold REAL interactive controls (buttons, links) as focusable siblings.

props.name: as
props.note: Root element. Default <ul>.

props.name: size
props.note: Row padding scale. Cascades to TListItem via the t-list--{size} class.

slots.name: default
slots.description: The TListItem rows.

a11y: plain_list_semantics: <ul>/<li> with no role override, so screen readers announce a list, not a listbox, and interactive controls in `actions` are reachable.
not_selectable: For a single-select list of options use TSelectableList instead.
behavior: responsive: On a container narrower than 30rem the row wraps: meta drops below the content while actions stay visible inline. Actions are NEVER hidden or auto-collapsed into an overflow menu — compose a TMenu inside the actions slot deliberately if an overflow is wanted (uses a CSS container query; the list sets container-type).
no_overflow_ownership: TList does not create, own, or collapse an actions overflow.

components.name: TMarkdownEditor
components.description: A Markdown editor with an always-visible side-by-side live preview, toolbar formatting, keyboard shortcuts, and image paste/drop upload support.

props.name: modelValue
props.description: Markdown content (v-model)

props.name: uploadImage
props.description: Async function that receives a File and returns the uploaded image URL. When not provided, pasting an image inserts an empty markdown image tag.

props.name: sanitize
props.description: Optional extra sanitizer applied to the rendered preview HTML as the final step, after the built-in rendering. It is an ADDITIVE escape hatch, not a toggle: pass DOMPurify or a stricter policy of your own instead of forking the component.

events.name: update:modelValue
events.description: Emitted on every content change for v-model binding.

events.name: image-upload
events.description: Emitted after a successful image upload with the file and resulting URL.

events.name: image-upload-error
events.description: Emitted when image upload fails.

slots.name: toolbar
slots.description: Extra content appended to the toolbar.

a11y: Toolbar has role="toolbar" with aria-label="Markdown formatting".
Toolbar groups are separated by elements with role="separator".
Upload status bar uses role="status" and aria-live="polite".
All toolbar buttons have title attributes describing the action.
Textarea has aria-label (mirrors the placeholder text).

useWhen: The user needs markdown formatting, toolbar actions, or preview.
avoidWhen: A plain multiline text area is enough.

alternatives.when: You only need multiline plain text.

components.name: TMenu
components.purpose: Rich action menu in a popover — slot-driven compound with groups, icons, descriptions, meta, destructive items, link items, and a radio (workspace-switch) item, with roving focus and keyboard support. Built on TPopover, so anchoring and focus restore come from there (TREEUX-015).

props.name: open
props.note: Controlled open state (v-model:open).

props.name: size
props.note: Forwarded to TPopover panel density.

props.name: label
props.note: Accessible name for the role=menu element.

props.name: closeOnSelect
props.note: Close the menu after an item is chosen. Closing routes through TPopover's close heuristic, so focus returns to the trigger.

slots.name: trigger
slots.description: Forwarded to TPopover's trigger slot ({ isOpen, contentId }).

slots.name: header
slots.description: Panel header, rendered OUTSIDE the role=menu element (so it is not a menu item).

slots.name: default
slots.description: TMenuGroup and TMenuItem children.

a11y: roles: role=menu on the list; role=menuitem / menuitemradio on items; role=group + aria-labelledby on groups. The header is outside role=menu.
roving_focus: One item holds tabindex=0, the rest -1. ArrowUp/Down move (skipping disabled), Home/End jump to edges, pointer focus syncs the active item.
escape_and_restore: Escape bubbles to the TPopover content, which closes and restores focus to the trigger. Selecting an item closes via the same TPopover close path, so focus returns to the trigger.
destructive_not_color_only: danger items still carry their text label, so meaning does not depend on colour alone; pair with a leading icon where helpful.

components.name: TModal
components.purpose: Accessible modal dialog for focused tasks and confirmations.

useWhen: The user must complete or review something in a blocking dialog.
avoidWhen: Only a small hint or contextual panel is needed.

alternatives.when: The dialog is specifically about confirm/cancel.
A side panel fits better.
An anchored non-blocking panel is enough.

components.name: TMultiSelect
components.purpose: Searchable multi-select control that renders selected values as tags.

props.name: options
props.note: Array of `{ label, value, description?, keywords?, disabled? }`.

useWhen: The user needs to select multiple values from a larger list.
Filtering and tag display improve the workflow.
avoidWhen: The option set is tiny and should always stay visible.

alternatives.when: The multiple-choice set is very small and visible.
The choices can stay visible as a checklist.
The choices are hierarchical.

Option search checks `label`, `description`, and `keywords`.
Use `maxVisibleTags` to control trigger density in compact layouts.

components.name: TNavbar
components.purpose: Top application bar with start, center, and end content areas.

Acts as a structural wrapper; semantics come from `as` and child content.

components.name: TNavMenu
components.purpose: Vertical navigation menu with selection state, keyboard navigation, optional RouterLink rendering, and sidebar integration.

props.name: items
props.note: Array of `{ label, value, shortLabel?, description?, icon?, to?, badge?, disabled?, exact? }`.

Register any icon the app needs beyond the built-ins first — see `icons` above.

components.name: TNumberInput

components.name: TPageHeader
components.purpose: Canonical page title block: heading, optional subtitle, and a right-aligned actions area, so title hierarchy and alignment stay consistent across screens.

Actions area holds standard focusable controls (e.g. TButton) — no custom roles added.

avoidWhen: You need cross-page hierarchy or navigation trails rather than a single page title.
